The English approached Bayeux and saw that its towering stone walls would not be breached by manpower alone.
Henry would need siege equipment, and a defensible location to construct it.
Henry now called for the services of a blacksmith to bolster the siege effort.
With a blacksmith on hand, Henry's infantry would soon be ready to construct siege equipment.
Henry's presence on the doorstep of Bayeux would not go unanswered and now his forces braced for an attack by Robert's army.
Henry repelled the attack, but he knew that so long as Bayeux still stood, the city would not give up the fight.
With the power of battering rams, the English army could crack open Bayeux's mighty walls.
Determined to claim Normandy and capture his brother, Duke Robert, King Henry besieged the castle at Tinchebray.
But the direct assault on the formidable stone castle was failing…
Unable to breach the towering walls, the king devised a new plan to capture his brother.
Henry would force Robert to defend his lands from attack by razing the villages of Martigny and Frênes.
Robert of Gloucester's forces approached Lincoln. Their task: to liberate the castle from King Stephen's siege…
…while behind Lincoln's walls, troops loyal to Matilda held the castle for the Empress.
First, Robert's forces would stage an ambush to prevent enemy reinforcements from joining with King Stephen's army.
Robert defeated the first column of Stephen's reinforcements, but he knew more were on the way.
With more of Stephen's reinforcements eliminated, Robert was steadily undermining the siege.
King Stephen had received some of his reinforcements and now Robert's men would face a stronger enemy in battle.
Robert's forces had failed to stop the reinforcements and would now face an army at full strength.
Robert's men decimated King Stephen's reinforcements, and were poised to break the siege.
Continuing the push towards Lincoln, Robert sent in a large company of reinforcements to aid his vanguard.
To further weaken the siege, Robert would need to cut off Stephen's supplies. He targeted the nearby market town.
Rallying to Matilda's cause, a large group of knights arrived at Lincoln to bolster Robert's army.
With the market town in ruins, King Stephen's supplies began to dwindle.
Robert's forces spotted a large detachment of Stephen's soldiers guarding the castle gates.
If they hoped to liberate Lincoln, they would need to break the blockade and rout the army.
Robert's forces scattered the king's army, leaving the gates to the castle unguarded.
Robert's men finally united with Lincoln's garrison. They could now use the castle to strengthen their army.
To lift the siege, they needed to destroy the enemy camps surrounding the castle.
Robert received word that Welsh troops under Matilda's banner were en route to join forces with him.
But King Stephen had the roads to Lincoln heavily guarded, and the route was not without risk, even for the hardy Welshmen.
Making their way to Lincoln, Matilda's Welsh allies were ambushed by the king's soldiers.
Joining forces on the road to Lincoln, Matilda's allied troops made for the castle to lift the siege.
Those loyal to Matilda had to ensure that the keep did not fall.
Mongke Khan called on his people to bring in their mobile camp, while his forces secured a location for it.
The khan was determined to punish the defiance of Kiev, and would not relent until the entire city fell to ash.
The Mongols took out a Kievan supply convoy and claimed the resources for themselves.
Mongke Khan and his Mongol warriors ran freely through the ravaged city, cutting down any survivors and plundering its riches.
With Kiev under Mongol control, the gateway to Europe was open, and the Empire could continue its conquest into the west.
The Jin fortress of Zhangjiakou blocked Genghis Khan’s path to the Great Wall.
Seeing the strength of the defenses at the front of the fortress, Genghis Khan dispatched his scouts to seek out an alternate approach.
Learning of the weak defenses at the back of the fortress, Genghis Khan saw his opportunity to attack. The Mongols began their ride to battle.
Next, the Mongols needed a base close to the Great Wall. They targeted the Jin village of Yanqing.
Villager now available! LEFT CLICK to produce one.
Commander Hubert de Burgh rallied his men to hold back the French and defend the castle.
A skilled bowman and fierce patriot known as Willikin of the Weald began to muster a resistance to the French invasion.
Determined to keep the crown in King John's hands, Willikin would rally every available archer to the cause.
With his bowmen assembled, Willikin planned to ambush the French siege engines on their way to the enemy camp.
Some French reinforcements made it to their siege camp, adding more numbers to their next attack on the castle.
Willikin's band of archers made short work of the French reinforcements.
But, if they hoped to ease the burden on Dover's garrison, Willikin's ambush force could not relent.
Willikin's resistance force continued to grow, adding more skilled men to its ranks.
Willikin spotted a group of English fighters engaged in battle with the enemy and rushed to their aid.
The French launched a renewed attack on the castle, but thanks to Willikin's ambush force, the enemy had been greatly weakened.
The French launched a renewed attack on the castle, but thanks to Willikin's ambush force, the enemy could not attack at full strength.
Bolstered by the addition of their reinforcements, the French committed the full force of their army to a second assault on the castle.
Willikin ordered his archers to resume their strikes on the French reinforcements.
Willikin's archers had decimated the French reinforcements. But, undeterred, the French mustered what troops they could and launched another attack on the castle.
Willikin's strikes had taken the sting out of the French army. But, determined to take the castle, the French launched a renewed attack.
Reinforced by their new arrivals, the French army was at full strength. Dover braced for a full-scale attack.

If the Mongols acted quickly they could defeat the scattered Polish army and force the Bohemians to withdraw.

To take the heavily garrisoned capital, the Mongols would have to starve its army by eliminating Zhongdu's suppliers.
But first, the khan directed his warriors to raid Jin buildings and villages surrounding Zhongdu.
Word of the army's wealth would spread, compelling Mongol soldiers from the north to join the fight, seeking spoils of their own.
Hearing of the khan's successful raids, Mongol settlers and soldiers arrived to join his army.
With his numbers steadily growing, the khan turned his attention to the capital. He sought to starve Zhongdu’s army – by cutting off its suppliers.
Genghis Khan’s army struck down the trader that was headed to resupply Zhongdu.
For every trader killed, Zhongdu lost several days' worth of supplies. The Mongols were systematically choking the city and starving its garrison.
In destroying the supplying markets, the Mongols denied Zhongdu critical supplies, leaving its garrison weakened.
The Mongols left the Shunyi market in ruins, ensuring the village could no longer send supplies to Zhongdu.
The market at Tongzhou was set aflame, and with it, many vital supplies intended for the city.
Without hesitation, the khan's warriors put their torches to the market at Daxing, snuffing out weeks of Zhongdu's supplies.
In razing the market at Fangshan, the Mongols had severed a critical supply line to the capital.
As the Mongols continued to sabotage the city's supplies, Zhongdu's garrison grew weaker by the day.
With Zhongdu devastated by starvation, the walls of the great Jin capital were devoid of soldiers and only a diminished garrison remained.
A Jin trader made it safely into the city and reported on the Mongols' numbers and movements.
Zhongdu would now allocate any military supplies it received to rallying counterattacks on the steppe invaders.
Despite the Mongols' efforts to stop them, enough Jin traders had entered Zhongdu to fully resupply its military. Choosing their moment, the Jin launched a counterattack.
The Mongols put down the Jin counterattack. But another would soon come if Jin traders continued to resupply Zhongdu's military.
At last, King Henry and his forces made their arrival at Fleury.

Villager Production - Ensure that you have enough Food and Houses so that you can produce Villagers at your Town Center without interruption.

Hunting - Villagers gather Food faster from Boar and Deer than they do from Sheep, Berries, and Farms.

Villager Assignment - Distribute your Villagers among Food, Wood, Stone, and Gold resources so that each objective completes around the same time.

Villagers harvest Food faster from Sheep and Farms than from Forage Bushes. Try using your Scout to search for Sheep and herd them back to a Town Center or Mill while your starting Villagers gather nearby resources.\r\n\r\nHunting is the fastest way to gather Food in the Dark Age. Boars are initially too dangerous to hunt, but Deer pose no threat to your Villagers and should be hunted early.

By destroying the Jin village, the Mongols had stopped the attacks from its garrison.
The Mongols eliminated Poland's knights, dealing a critical blow to the European forces.
The Mongols defeated the Polish detachment and continued their unrelenting drive.
The Mongols cut down more Polish fighters, further eroding Europe's hope of slowing the invasion.
Before the battle, Dmitry would recruit soldiers from nearby villages.
The villages pledged all the soldiers they could spare, strengthening Dmitry's position against the Horde.
The Rus discovered a new settlement, which pledged its soldiers to Moscow's cause.
Dmitry delivered the battle orders to his allies: they would take up position on the Smolka river fords and ready themselves for the enemy’s arrival.
The cavalry detachment was in position and waited for Dmitry’s signal to strike.
Running out of time, Dmitry ordered his army to hold back the Mongols long enough for Moscow's citizens to escape.
The Rus were unable to eliminate all the Lithuanian traders; the well-supplied fortress launched an attack.
He would recapture the border towns and force a surrender from the French king.
Robert’s men concealed themselves in a sheltered grove and lay in wait for the enemy.
Robert's men would need to aid the Welsh if they wanted their help in breaking the siege.
He would also ride to his allies who were awaiting their battle orders.

As they retreated northwards, a French vanguard was in close pursuit.
A detachment of English archers blocked the road with palings, an effective measure against cavalry.
The French vanguard had to clear the road if they were to call in reinforcements.
The French recaptured St. Sigmund, and prepared to rendezvous with Jeanne d'Arc.
But a large English detachment had set up a blockade.
So long as that force was in place, the French vanguard could not reach Jeanne.
With the English blockade destroyed, the French vanguard rejoiced at the arrival of Jeanne d'Arc and her troops.
With their rearguard eliminated, the remaining English forces would now seek to escape the valley through the town of Patay.
Three enemy regiments were converging on the town along three main roads.
Jeanne had either to stop the regiments on the roads, or race ahead to fortify Patay itself.
As the forces of the valiant Jeanne d'Arc entered the town, the people of Patay rallied to her cause.
The town would not hold out long without help.

The Mongols unpacked their camp, ready to continue their assault on Kiev.
The immediate area beyond Kiev's main gate was lightly populated but rich in resources.
Beyond, lay three fortified districts: the outer city, the inner city, and a wealthy trade district.
As the last buildings collapsed, the Mongol victory was complete: Kiev had fallen.

Hostile archers defended the next camp, which would put spearmen at a disadvantage.
Fortunately, friendly villagers came out of hiding and joined the effort.
Reduce the reload time of Arbalétriers by %1% seconds.
With several archery ranges in place, the town could add longbowmen to its forces.
Once more, friendly villagers arrived to help.

With no word from their Lithuanian allies, the Mongols called in their own reinforcements to bolster their numbers on the fords.
If Ivan's forces could intercept and eliminate the detachment, the Rus would face less resistance on the fords.
Ivan's men cut down the Mongol reinforcements, ensuring they could not bolster the enemy's numbers on the fords.
The Mongol reinforcements made it to the enemy side of the Ugra river, and soon they would bolster the Mongol numbers on the fords.
Ivan's Rus army stood strong at the Ugra crossings and would not allow the Horde to advance towards Moscow.
Dmitry and his Rus allies held the fords and eliminated the Mongol vanguard.
While the enemy regrouped after the initial skirmish, the Prince ordered the Rus to fall back to the hilltop defenses behind them.
As they retreated, nearby Rus provinces mustered additional forces to Dmitry's side.
Ivan IV's ability can increase the reload speed of nearby gunpowder units

Tokhtamysh Khan sacked the city, plundering its riches and slaughtering any Muscovites that remained.
Prince Dmitry had no choice but to take flight, abandoning his home.
Rus independence would have to wait...
Dmitry and his men held the city, giving some of Moscow’s citizens time to flee.
But despite their steadfast defense, the Rus could hold out no longer and the capital succumbed to the vast numbers of the Horde.
Batu Khan led his warriors across the plains, luring the Hungarian king to a battle site far from the safety of his city.
As the Hungarians broke away to regroup across the Sajo river, the Mongols prepared to strike.
General Subutai would soon arrive to join Batu, having escorted the Mongol camp to the battle site.
While the khan waited for his general, the Mongol vanguard would probe across the bridge to the north.
Across the bridge, the Mongol soldiers found themselves heavily outnumbered and beat a hasty retreat away from the ambush.
Arriving at the battle site, General Subutai quickly assessed the situation.
This was not the first time Batu had been outnumbered. Drawing on past victories, he began to stage a coordinated attack.
The Hungarians saw a chance to strike and made their move where the Mongols were vulnerable.
The Mongols took the bridge and held the open space, forcing the Hungarians into the trees, and splitting them into several groups.
Now the Mongols could drive the Hungarians back to their camp – one small space with nowhere to run.
With the Hungarians pinned down, the Mongols closed in to destroy the last of their army.

The Chinese can shift their focus across the ages, deploying many unique units and building rapidly. Enemies must continually adapt if they want to keep up.
The French deploy powerful cavalry units and can boost production in fortified positions. Enemies must be prepared to withstand the charges of powerful Royal Knights and other armored units.

Prelates enhance the economy of the Holy Roman Empire, while powerful infantry units form the core of its military. Enemies must face an opponent able to rapidly recover from attacks and field strong counterattacks.

Masters of mobility and mounted warfare, the Mongols can easily relocate their camps. They gain economic benefits from setting up near Stone Outcroppings and from raiding enemy buildings. Enemies must deal with cavalry attacks from the opening moments of play.

The Rus derive great benefit from the countryside. They are able to gather resources more readily from hunting and forestry and can field strong combat units. Enemies face strong early game fortifications and a diverse economy that cannot easily be disrupted.

Ivan had prepared resources to rapidly construct a base on the Volga River, and his navy now delivered these.
Now Ivan would need to defend it while building up a siege force with which to breach Kazan's walls.
Ivan's army pushed across the Volga.
They could expect stiff Tatar resistance.
Muscovite forces moved into the Tatar pasture lands. They were getting close to Kazan proper.
Ivan's men were finally in sight of Kazan's walls.
It was here previous sieges had failed, but Ivan swore this time would be different.
Kazan's inner wall crumbled before Ivan's forces.
Within the inner city, the Khan's guard awaited.
With a mighty warcry, the Khan's elite guard set upon Ivan's forces.
He ordered his army to the twin cities of Fancheng and Xiangyang, confident in his tried and tested tactics.
The Mongols spotted a fortified Song camp, blocking the road to the twin cities.
Seeing an opportunity to overwhelm the enemy camp, the Mongols called in their reinforcements.
Together, the two detachments would strike the camp from both sides, pincering the Song.
This assault force would attack the gates of Fancheng, while the Mongol vanguard would defend the siege weapons firing upon the cities.
As the allied force charged the gates, the Mongol vanguard moved to defend their siege weapons from Song attacks.
As the Mongols approached, the Song burned the bridge to Xiangyang, preventing a direct assault on the fortress.
The assault on Fancheng was met with a barrage of gunfire, forcing the allied army to retreat.
With the gates still unbroken and under guard, the Mongols switched strategies, and called on a large group of reinforcements to bring in their mobile camp.
The Mongols would besiege the mighty twin cities.
The Mongols rallied a force on the bridgehead.
The Mongol strategy was to hold the city’s bridges so that no Song forces could escape, and no reinforcements could enter.
Securing the second bridgehead, the Mongols were one step closer to blocking off all the escape routes.
The Mongols took up position on the last bridgehead.
Seeing that the Mongols were attempting to block the city's escape routes, the Song began planning a counterattack.
The Mongols had to hold the three bridgeheads against the Song attacks.
Desperate to stop an all out Mongol assault on their walls, the Song destroyed the remaining bridges to the twin cities.
Unable to advance, the fortress of Xiangyang remained locked to the Mongols.

The general's plan depended on recruiting local workers who could build defenses.
The first step was to eliminate the Song garrison near the village of Lumen.
The Mongol army tore through the Song garrison, and with these guardians removed, the people of Lumen gave their support to Liu Zheng and Kublai Khan.
Controlling Lumen allowed Liu Zheng to employ Chinese tactics and equipment, including the construction of stone walls.
He ordered the people to build walls to block the roads north, cutting off Xiangyang from the rest of the Song.
The people of Lumen reported that the village of Bohekou, located to the west, would also welcome Liu Zheng's arrival.
If the general could get forces to Bohekou, it would add both an economic and military boost to his blockade.
Bohekou not only welcomed Liu Zheng's men, but members of the local militia even defected to the Mongol cause.
Song forces descended on Liu Zheng's men. The general needed to defend what he had taken from his former masters.
Stout walls now blocked off the first of the major routes to Xiangyang.

If Orléans hoped to receive the supplies, the French would have to clear the road of English raiders.
The French eliminated the English raiders, granting the supply convoy safe passage into the city.
Each time supplies approached Orléans, English raiders would return to guard the road, hoping to plunder the French goods.
The supply convoy arrived in the city, providing the French army with much needed gold.
Seeing another supply convoy approaching the city, English raiders took up position on the road.
The French needed to eliminate the enemy detachment if they hoped to receive the supplies.
Another trade convoy arrived in Orléans with more gold to bolster the army.
The northern fort fell to the French, reducing the enemy presence at Orléans.
The French army torched the eastern English fort, securing a crossing to the south bank of the Loire river.
Jeanne d'Arc's army burned the northwest English fort, relieving pressure on Orléans.
Jeanne d'Arc and her army had eliminated all the English forts on the north side of the Loire river, giving the French respite from the frequent attacks on the city.
In order to fully liberate Orléans, Jeanne d'Arc would need to destroy the English keep at Les Tourelles.
With the western fort destroyed, the French could safely cross the Loire river by this route.
The last obstacle to Les Tourelles fell to ruin.

King Charles VII's army would soon have access to the most advanced artillery in Europe, thanks to the master gunners, the Bureau Brothers.
While the brothers developed their weapons, the king laid plans for their use: he would bring Normandy back under French control.
The first phase of the king's plan would give the Bureau Brothers time to perfect their cannons.
He tasked his army with claiming a base of operations.
With the town under French control, the Bureau Brothers now had the use of the university and of their own siege workshop.
With the town under French control, the Bureau Brothers now had access to all the research and development resources they needed.
Their next goal was to learn all they could about recent innovations in the mixing of the most potent gunpowder.
While the army waited for their new weapons, they kept a watchful eye for any English attacks on the town.
The brothers worked tirelessly to cast their gun barrels, but they still needed insight into the latest gunpowder chemistry.
The Bureau Brothers had successfully combined a powerful gunpowder recipe with sturdy cast iron gun barrels.
While the brothers turned out their cannons, culverins and ribauldequins, the French army would bolster themselves for the coming fight.
While the brothers turned out their guns, the French army would capture key locations and bolster themselves for the coming fight.
With the skirmish won, trade could begin with the settlement.
With the mine under French control, the Bureau Brothers had access to an abundance of iron for their gun barrels.
After razing the river port, the French soldiers seized a stockpile of ship-worthy timber, which could be used for superior cannon frames.
At last, the French artillery weapons were ready for deployment.
After years of preparation, the time had come for Charles VII to make his move.
To take back Normandy he would need to recapture the stronghold of Rouen, a critical fortress from which the English had been attacking Paris.
Rouen's walls would face an explosive trial against the mighty force of gunpowder.
A party of English soldiers approached the French base, intent on reclaiming the town.
An English assault struck at the heart of the French town, threatening the entire operation.
If the base fell, the French would have to withdraw from Normandy.
Rouen's walls were breached. The French army had at last exposed the heart of the fortress.
Driven from their homes by armed invaders, a few hardy refugees faced the prospect of starting again.
They would found a new village deep in the countryside.
With a new settlement established, the first priority was locating a reliable food source.
The simplest source was gathering from nature.
With a healthy supply of food, the village could start to grow.
First, they would build a mill near their food source so villagers could drop off gathered berries more easily.
But a populous village would soon exhaust the natural food sources.
The townsfolk had been driven from their homes before, however; this time they would defend themselves.
The town now had a militia and could look to reclaim the lands lost to invasion.
The invaders had blocked the road north with a stout palisade.
With the road open, the militia could now reclaim their lands in the north.
The invaders had a small cavalry camp guarding the road, but the militia was ready to attack.
All that remained was to destroy the invaders' stables.
The invaders' cavalry post was destroyed, but other enemy positions awaited further up the road.
To deploy that cavalry quickly, the town needed to build their stables near the front lines.
The invaders' archers and their camp were destroyed.
First, they needed to build archery ranges in the area regained from the invaders.
Poised on a clifftop, the longbowmen would be protected from an infantry charge.
The last of the invaders fell to the resurgent homegrown population.
Now that their lands were free of enemies, the town could take the next step in its growth and become a powerful medieval city.
Where a few lowly refugees had founded a small village, now rose a mighty city.
From there, would grow an empire.

The Muscovite army destroyed the outer wall and entered the city.
Ivan's forces captured the second town, and laid a plan to secure Novgorod for Moscow.
Northeast of Cocherel, Charles of Navarre's army had established a fortified camp.
The King of Navarre sought to make himself ruler of France as well.
He intended to press his claim by destroying the newly arrived royal forces.

During the years-long siege, Kublai Khan had established lucrative trade routes with Chinese market towns who swore loyalty to him.
Although these trade partners were vulnerable to Song attacks, they could deliver valuable resources to fund Ismail's efforts.
The Mongol commercial network could supply Ismail in stone so long as he had enough traders on the road.
Song forces destroyed one of the market towns trading with Kublai Khan's army.
Only one trade route remained open to the Mongols.
The Song burned the second market, cutting off all trade supplying the Mongols.

Intent on ending the suffering of the French peasantry, Sir Jean de Beaumanoir sent a challenge to the English commander.
With the battleground of the Halfway Oak agreed upon, Sir Jean set out to gather support from local knights.
As a knight himself, Sir Jean followed the strict rules of chivalry and was expected to protect the local peasantry and ensure peace.
The two knights struck down the wolves and Sir Olivier Arrel joined Sir Jean on the road to the Halfway Oak.
Most medieval tournaments were friendly in nature – held for sport and glory – but the Combat of the Thirty was arranged between opponents mired in war.
Sir Jean spotted an English raiding party attacking a nearby farmstead.
The French knights defeated the English raiders, and Sir Guy de Rochefort joined Sir Jean's party.
English raids had torn through the countryside of Brittany and brought great hardship to the people.
Sir Jean's search next brought him to Sir Geoffroy du Bois, whose squires were contending with a detachment of English longbowmen.
The knights would use the great speed of their warhorses to charge the archers and overwhelm them.
The English longbowmen fell, but the French knights knew the enemy would attempt to retake the hilltop fortification, and steeled themselves for further attacks.
With Sir Geoffroy by his side, Sir Jean had secured the outpost.
Looking to secure his honor, a young knight held his ground on a bridge in Sir Jean's path.
Honorably conceding defeat in the duel, Sir Yves Charruel joined the cause.
The French knights now made for the Halfway Oak – a patch of neutral ground where the tournament would be held.
Sir Jean entered the staging area for the tournament, where he prepared to choose which knights would join him in battle.
Each knight would be accompanied by his squires – young nobles in training to become knights themselves.
Sir Jean had selected his champions.
As the sun rose, the two sides entered the arena, ready for the first round of combat.
English fighters who fell in combat would be ransomed by the French for large sums of gold.
The French were at a disadvantage following the first round, but they would use the break to strengthen their forces.
Refreshed and reinvigorated, the French knights returned to the arena for another round of combat.
The Combat of the Thirty was about to decide which nation would control the Duchy of Brittany.
Triumphant, Sir Jean de Beaumanoir and his loyal knights claimed victory.
At last, cannon production could begin.
The brothers now had every available advantage in producing their guns.
Liu Zheng's construction skill can help infantry bridge the moat faster.

Prepare to advance to the Feudal Age
When the game begins, you must produce new Villagers to rapidly grow your settlement.
To support them, first focus on putting your existing Villagers to work gathering Food from nearby Sheep.
While your Villagers are working, use your Scout to explore the surrounding area and locate additional resources.
When you find more Sheep, use your Scout to herd them back to your town so that your Villagers always have Food to gather.
A growing town needs more buildings to support its residents.
Construct additional Houses to increase your population capacity.
Place economic buildings near resources so that your Villagers gather more efficiently and waste less time walking.
You can also improve your Villagers' gathering rates by researching new technologies at your economic buildings.
Survival Techniques, for example, allows your Villagers to hunt faster and carry more Food.
When you have enough Villagers, advance to the next Age by constructing a Landmark.
Each Landmark provides a different set of benefits, so choose wisely.
When your Landmark is complete, your village will spring into the next Age, ready to keep growing.
To complete this challenge, you must produce many Villagers and gather enough Food and Gold to place a Landmark and advance to the Feudal Age. Good luck!
Start by ordering your existing Villagers to gather Food from Sheep, then queue up additional Villagers at your Town Center.
Use your Scout to explore the surrounding area and locate additional resources.
Your Villagers should chop some Wood. Send one of your Villagers to build a Lumber Camp near the closest forest.
Your Villagers need Houses. Build a House each time that you have two or less remaining population capacity.
Sheep will not fill your Food stockpiles forever. Build a Mill next to the nearby herd of Deer and begin hunting.
Your other Food gatherers can join the hunters once all the Sheep are gone.
Hunting exposes your Villagers to raids, but Deer are a faster source of Food than Farms, Berry Bushes, or Sheep.
Well done! Your Mill is complete.
Consider researching Survival Techniques to make your hunters more effective.
Now is a good time to begin mining Gold.
Use a Villager to build a Mining Camp near the closest Gold Vein.
Remember to keep producing new Villagers.
Order new Villagers to mine Gold as well.
You do not need to mine Stone during this challenge.
You have enough Villagers. Start building a Landmark once you have gathered enough Food and Gold.
You have enough resources to construct a Landmark, but you also need more Villagers to finish this challenge.
You have gathered the resources and produced the Villagers necessary to advance with strength.
Now, use your Villagers to place a Landmark to advance to the Feudal Age and complete this challenge.
Well done! You have quickly built up your economy and are ready for the next Age.
Once your Scout finds a herd of Deer, order your Villagers to hunt them.
Send your Scout south to find the Deer closest to your Town Center.
Build a Landmark and advance to the Imperial Age
When you are in a safe position, you can focus on your economy instead of your military.
This is risky, but if successful you can accumulate large amounts of resources with which to crush your enemies.
Start by queuing up Villagers at your Town Center and Traders at your Market, then order your idle Villagers to gather resources.
How to divide your gatherers between Food, Wood, Gold, and Stone is something that you must figure out for yourself – but try to focus on long-term goals and reassign your Villagers when necessary.
A single Town Center and Market take a long time to produce the required number of Villagers and Traders, so you will need several more.
Make sure that both building types are always producing units. A successful economic boom relies on swiftly producing many Villagers and Traders.
Research economic technologies to make your Villagers as productive as possible.
These increase your gathering rates and are crucial to your success.
Now it is up to you. Use the lessons that you have learned here and build up your empire!
To complete this challenge, you must produce a large number of Villagers and Traders and advance to the Imperial Age by constructing a Landmark.
Your first task is to make sure that your Town Center and Market are producing Villagers and Traders.
It is very important that these buildings are never idle.
You now have the resources to construct a second Town Center.
Build it near resource deposits so that your Villagers have easy access to them.
Excellent! Once you have the resources, consider building a third and maybe even a fourth Town Center.
Now is a good time to build extra Markets to produce more Traders.
Traders are crucial to a booming economy because they boost your Gold income and are produced from a different building than Villagers — so you can produce both at the same time.
You need additional Houses to produce more units.
Build extra Houses to increase your population capacity.
You will need a lot of Food to produce enough Villagers and advance to the next Age.
Build many Farms to ensure a steady Food supply.

Very good! You have trained enough Villagers and Traders.

They are produced from different buildings, armed with different weapons, and specialized against different targets.
Spearmen carry long polearms into battle.
Archers perform very well against unarmored infantry due to their long range.
Notice how they are concealed among the trees — positioning units in a Stealth Forest keeps them hidden until they start attacking.
Look! A group of Horsemen was hiding out of view behind that ridge.
These archers stand no chance against quick and mobile cavalry.
Remember these lessons and apply them.
Choose the right counters to enemy forces and use the terrain to your advantage to dominate the battlefield.
A group of Horsemen is about to attack your village.
Four more waves of enemies will soon follow.
Send your Scout south to find the Deer closest to your Town Center.
You must defend the three entrances to the village and prevent the enemy from burning down your buildings.
You can only deploy one type of unit at a time, so choose wisely.
Select one of the allied buildings in the center of the village to deploy a group of units.
Here comes the enemy! Ready your units!
Now a group of enemy Spearmen is coming from the north!
Choose the right unit type to counter them before they reach the village.
See this sparse forest near the road?
You can use it to hide your units and ambush your enemies as they pass.
Those raiders won't trouble you again!
Another enemy band is attacking from the south! This time, they are bringing Archers.
Choose some defenders and face them head-on!
Those Archers have shot their last volley.
Beware, the enemy is now attacking in force!
They are more determined than ever and are bringing both Horsemen AND Spearmen.
This time, you can choose two types of units with which to repel the invaders.
It is a good idea to use melee infantry as a shield to block incoming enemies and protect your vulnerable ranged units.
Use control groups to manage your forces.
Excellent! Those raiders were no match for you.
A group of Archers and Horsemen is launching the final attack from the south.
Select two types of units to defend your village.
Look closely — you can hide some troops behind that ridge on the flank.
Use it to ambush and divide the invaders.
Well done! You have repulsed the attacks and kept your village safe.
Enemy soldiers have looted some of your Houses!
LEFT CLICK to train a group of Spearmen.

LEFT CLICK to train a group of Horsemen.
Sometimes, your opponent will build walls to ward off attacks.
In this challenge, you will learn how to use siege weapons to destroy a fortified camp.
Use Battering Rams to tear down fortifications. Once you research Siege Engineering, your soldiers can build them in the field.
You can garrison several units inside a single ram to keep them safe from arrow fire.
Springalds are great for fighting enemy units, but ineffective against walls.
Mangonels are moderately effective against buildings but less so against walls.
They deal damage over a wide area, however, and are excellent against groups of foot soldiers.
Remember what you have learned during this introduction. Construct the proper siege weapons to use against your enemies' fortifications and defending forces.
With superior siege tactics and equipment, no walls can hold against you.
Your goal in this challenge is to destroy the camp of your Rus opponent, who shelters behind thick palisade walls.
Use the destructive power of your siege weapons to level the enemy encampment.
Every siege unit is specialized, and some may not be suitable for the task at hand.
Siege Towers, for example, are irrelevant in this challenge because the enemy does not have stone walls.
Enemy patrols guard the camp. Use Springalds or Mangonels to help your soldiers eliminate these defenders.
Use rams to batter down your opponent's walls and destroy their buildings.
Your soldiers have broken into the enemy camp!
Do not let up. Destroy the enemy Landmark and Town Center to claim victory.
Flattening an enemy base is satisfying, but prioritizing the Town Center and Landmark will yield a swifter victory.
The enemy Town Center lies in ashes. They will not recover from this loss!
You have destroyed the enemy Landmark!
The Rus camp was no match for your clever use of siege weapons. Good work!
Springalds are specialized at taking out enemy units but are weak against walls and buildings.
Mangonels are good against groups of slow-moving soldiers.
Garrison infantry inside of this Ram to protect them from arrow fire.
Siege Towers are only effective against stone walls, not palisades.
Select a military unit to build a Battering Ram.
Click here to build a Battering Ram.
Click a valid location to start construction.
Destroy the Holy Roman Empire's Town Center
When facing enemy walls or defensive buildings, you need siege engines to win the day.
As early as the Feudal Age, your infantry can construct Battering Rams and Siege Towers.
While not as powerful as the siege engines of later Ages, they can still sometimes be the best choice.
Battering Rams can protect infantry from arrow fire and punch holes in enemy fortifications.
Siege Towers allow you to take control of enemy walls without reducing them to rubble.
However, both of these weapons must travel to their targets, exposing them to enemy attacks.
In the Imperial Age, you can construct more powerful engines from the Siege Workshop.
Trebuchets and Bombards can tear down gates and buildings from a distance.
They can only fire in certain arcs and on certain trajectories, however, so position them carefully.
They are also expensive, so guard them well.
You must choose the best weapons to employ for the task at hand.
Your opponent seeks refuge behind thick stone walls.
To triumph, use siege weapons that can overcome these fortifications!
Your resources are limited, so think carefully before deciding which siege engines to deploy!
You can also research additional siege technologies at the Madrasa, the Delhi Sultanate's version of the University.
Every second spent waiting for these technologies to complete, however, is a second lost towards earning a medal!
Your enemies are now sallying forth from their walls!
With its walls in ruins, the heart of the city is now vulnerable.
The enemy Town Center lies within range!
Your men have scaled the walls of the city!
The enemy archers can no longer fire down upon you.
The enemy Town Center lies in ruins!
You have razed the first enemy Landmark!
You have destroyed the second enemy Landmark!
The enemy Town Center and Landmarks are obliterated! Well done!
Springalds are strong against units, but will not make much of a dent in walls.
Siege Towers can be garrisoned and used to scale enemy stone walls.
Battering Rams are strong against walls and buildings, but do not have the benefit of a ranged weapon.
Mangonels are strong against groups of units, but weak against walls and buildings.
Trebuchets have very long range, fire in an arc, and are strong against walls and buildings.
Bombards have long range, fire on a linear trajectory, and are strong against walls, buildings, and units.

PH - Trade ships need to target coastal trade settlements or allied docks, and will then travel back and forth to generate gold
PH - Traders and Trade Ships that belong to the French civilization are able to choose the resource they generate
PH - Attack Ships are the first and cheapest military ships available.
PH - Their initial weapons fire broadside at a moderate range, but upgrades increase their flexibility.
PH - Demolition Ships don't attack directly, but explode in a radius for fire damage when they are destroyed.
PH - They can be devastating to a massed fleet, but can be destroyed from a distance by concentrated fire.
PH - War Galleys, like the French Galleass, are more expensive ships that attack at longer ranges than the Attack Ship.
PH - Transport ships are required to carry land units across water to attack
PH - Land units can be loaded into a transport ship by moving the units and targeting the ship
PH - While aboard a ship, the units will be vulnerable - if the transport ship is destroyed, all units inside will be lost as well
PH - Transport Ships carrying units can unload themselves onto any valid shoreline

Rams are armored against ranged attacks, but can easily be burned by torches.
Siege Towers are tall enough to unload infantry onto your walls, who can then make quick work of your light ranged defenders.
Mangonels are highly effective against groups of enemy units. They can also do a small amount of damage to Stone Walls.
Advanced siege weapons, like Trebuchets and Cannons, can cause devastating damage from long range.
Your walls are damaged! Use Villagers to repair them and deny the enemy a weak point.
Building Stone Wall Towers at likely siege points discourages close range attacks.
Available from the Feudal Age, Stone Walls are an effective defensive measure against approaching armies.
Infantry units cannot directly attack stone walls or gates, and are vulnerable to ranged attacks from units on or behind the walls.
Stone Wall Towers can be built into walls at crucial chokepoints, and upgraded with Springalds or Cannons. They will attack any units that come into range.
Powerful upgrades, like Boiling Oil, can further improve your wall defenses, damaging all units in close range of Towers and Keeps.
Siege weapons, like the Ram, are highly effective at attacking buildings, and can directly attack Stone Walls.
They're armored against basic ranged attacks, but melee attackers can take them out quickly with torches.
Ranged siege weaponry like Mangonels, Trebuchets, Springalds and Cannons allow the enemy to attack walls from a distance.
They are likewise vulnerable to fire from torches, but beware - longer ranged weapons will outrange your wall defenses, and can open your sallying army to counterattack.
Like other weapons, Siege Towers are vulnerable to fire attacks from torches.
They are slow to approach, but if they reach your walls, they can unload enemy units directly onto the walls, leaving any ranged units on top vulnerable to melee combat.

As a game progresses, you will gain access to increasingly powerful troops.
Heavily armored Knights, Crossbowmen, and Men-at-arms will tend to form the core of your armies.
However, you will still find use for lighter troops in a supporting role.
Men-at-arms, for instance, are slow and heavy units with significant armor.
They can easily defeat any lighter unit on the battlefield.
Crossbowmen have a powerful ranged attack that makes quick work of light infantry and does additional damage to heavy units.
If you are fielding an infantry force and see many Crossbowmen in your enemy’s army, retreat to preserve your force.
Heavy melee cavalry, such as Knights, repel most attacks and can charge at speed, inflicting heavy damage and throwing opponents into disarray.
Here, the Knights force the Crossbowmen to engage unfavorably and quickly cut them down.
Watch how these knights engage the crossbowmen and quickly cut them down.
However, as with regular Horsemen, Knights should steer away from enemy Spearmen.
In most battles, you will face armies comprised of many different unit types.
Deploy a combined force of your own, choosing units that counter those of the enemy, and look for opportunities to draw them in.
Remember what you have learned here and add it to your arsenal of battle tactics.
Your soldiers have spotted an army marching towards your village.
The first group of attackers that will reach you is comprised of Men-at-arms but five more waves of enemies will soon follow.
Select one of the production buildings in the center of the village to deploy a group of units.

It is a good idea to use infantry as a shield to block incoming enemies and protect your backline of vulnerable ranged units.
Assign different groups of units to Control Groups to more easily manage them.
The impassable cliffs next to the road allow your ranged units to fire down on enemies without opening themselves up to immediate counterattack.
The natural choke point leading up to the cliffs is a good spot to position your infantry and protect your ranged units.
Well done! You have defeated the Men-at-arms.
Excellent! Your village is safe from the Crossbowmen.
You have shown that with the right strategy, even hardy Knights can be defeated.
By using a combination of unit types, you have outmaneuvered the enemy.
Good work! The enemy raiders could not overcome your superior army.
Victory! You have defeated the enemy and secured your village.

The Ottoman Army can expand to dominate the battlefield, supported by Military Schools that are able to continuously produce units to swell the ranks of their formations. Supporting those formations, the Mehter war drums signal the highly trained troops to take situational advantage.
Military production buildings constructed within the influence of a Blacksmith or University gain increased production speed with each age up.

Sipahi: Horseman replacement. Activate the Fortitude ability to deal extra damage to ranged units but receive more damage from melee weapons.

Mehter: Mounted war drummer that provides military enhancements to nearby units when toggling the War Drums ability.
• Attack Drums: Increased attack speed.
• Melee Defense Drums: Increased melee armor.
• Ranged Defense Drums: Increased ranged armor.

Janissary: Powerful handcannoneer that can repair siege engines and deal bonus damage to cavalry.

Grand Galley: Large war galley that can convert into a floating Military School.

Great Bombard: Largest gunpowder siege engine that can be fielded. Has long range and area damage attack.

Akinji: Long-ranged cavalry which fires two arrows in quick succession. Must be unlocked through the Imperial Council.

• Training units and advancing the ages grant experience towards Vizier Points, the higher cost of the action, the more experience is earned. Gain up to five Vizier Points and spend them to unlock powerful unique Ottoman bonuses.
• Military Schools can produce units continuously at no cost.
• Influence from Blacksmiths and Universities provide increased military unit production speeds after each age up.
• Military and technology buildings are cheaper.
• Janissary handcannoneer unit available in the Castle Age (III). Able to repair siege engines.
• Mehter war drummer enhances the attack and defenses of nearby military units.
• Field the Great Bombard siege engine with longer range and area damage.
• Produce the Grand Galley ship that can convert into a floating Military School.
• Movement speed of Trade Ships and Transport Ships increased.
• Cannot harvest Boar.

• Donso: Spearman replacement. Light melee infantry with increased health and melee armor that can periodically throw a javelin.
• Warrior Scout: Light cavalry that can improve damage, move speed, health, and health regeneration.

• Javelin Thrower: Anti-ranged infantry that throws a javelin from extended range.
• Sofa: Faster heavy cavalry with reduced cost, health, and armor.
• Musofadi Gunner: Faster moving handcannoneer that can use the Activate Stealth ability.

While the crow's nest served the valuable purpose of land-spotting, it was sometimes used as punishment. The movement of the seas from atop the famed vantage point was strong enough to induce seasickness!
While these have been commonly associated with pirates, these prosthetics were used by anyone with access to scrap and salvage.
Swordfish are an elusive, impressive fish that use their sword to slash at prey. They travel alone and migrate over long distances, making them a rare sight.
They can be found in the deepest reaches of the ocean, or dancing to party music on sandy shores.
Some believe the manatee of all creatures to be the inspiration for tales of mermaids and sirens.

From the depths of the sea, the oarfish looks as if it hails from a different planet entirely.\r\n\r\nIt can reach up to 56 feet long and weigh 600 pounds. There is no surprise that it may have sprouted tales of sea serpents across the world.
A creature so large and alien, it is no surprise that giant squids instilled such apprehension and respect in hardened seafarers.

In antiquity, one school of thought was that every land animal had a counterpart in the ocean. It's why we ended up with names like "sea pig" or "frogfish" for sea creatures that are not at all related to their namesakes.\r\n\r\nGranted for participating in a ranked season.
Most commonly depicted as just a head above water, it leaves one to wonder what lurks in the depths below.\r\n\r\nGranted for finishing Silver or above in a ranked season.
Sirens were thought to entice sailors with beauty and song, only to lure them to their untimely demise.\r\n\r\nGranted for finishing Gold or above in a ranked season.

Talkative and inquisitive, parrots of many varieties could be staunch companions during long voyages.
Aurora borealis are a rare and mesmerizing sight.
No adventure was complete without the navigational aid of a compass.
Bioluminescence is a phenomenon of the deep seas, where animals glow for a variety of ecological purposes.
While a single dolphin may have been cause for mistaken identity, it is more likely that a massive pod on the distant horizon could have appeared to be something bigger.
Eels washed ashore could have understandably been thought of as baby sea serpents, furthering the idea that these creatures were real and terrifying.
Sailors would sometimes use seagulls or other birds to spot land before a coastline could be seen on the horizon.

The legends and folklore of the water-horse is muddy at best, with confusion over their preferred hunting grounds - lakes or seas.

One of the most commonly depicted map monsters, the sea serpent makes its appearance on most of the world's oceans.\r\n\r\nThought to inhabit stormy seas, these creatures would lurk in wait of passing ships.

Perhaps the most feared monster of all, the terrifying Kraken plagued the nightmares of adventurers across the world. These massive leviathans could play with ships like bath toys.\r\n\r\nGranted for finishing Conqueror in a ranked season.

Musofadi Gunners excel at hit-and-run tactics - and their ambush damage when attacking from a stealth forest can be the start of a favorable fight. First-strike ambush bonus provides will be applied to stats.
Throw all three Festivals with the Griot Bara in Age IV.

The Ottomans can garrison Traders within their Sultanhani Trade Network Landmark to produce Gold. The Ottomans take time to reach their stride, so an early economic advantage is crucial.
Cultivation of the surrounding lands was a staple of Ottoman supremacy. An additional Food source can make the difference in military production as you reach Age II.
Sipahis were renowned for their agility and maneuverability. This especially works in their favor when flanking enemy ranged units.
Defeat 10 ranged enemies with Sipahis affected by Fortitude.
Research 5 Imperial Council bonuses before reaching Age IV.

You can gain experience towards Viziers, which allows you to research unique upgrades with the Imperial Council. Not all bonuses can be achieved in a single game, so you will need to prioritize accordingly.
Have 10 Mehters playing their war drums simultaneously.
Mehters can apply unique bonuses. Choosing the right bonus at the right time can be the difference between victory and defeat.

Construct at least 3 Military Schools and produce 100 units from them.
The Military School can produce units passively, allowing you to focus your economy more freely.
Janissaries are adept at picking off enemy cavalry, but their ability to repair siege units in the field should not be discounted.
Research the Imperial Fleet upgrade, then construct 10 Cannon Ships.
The Imperial Fleet upgrade, researched in the Docks, reduces the train time and improves the move speed of cannon ships.

Construct the Sea Gate Castle Landmark and have 5 Keeps providing Trade Protection.
All Ottoman Castles provide this bonus to Traders and Trade Ships, and the Sea Gate Castle Landmark is no exception.
Construct the Istanbul Observatory then produce 100 units.
Universities provide an increase to unit production speed for nearby military production buildings. This Landmark, however, further increases that bonus.
Destroy 3 enemy Keeps with the Great Bombard.
The Great Bombard has unparalleled damage at a far range. However, it is revealed to enemies when it prepares to fire. Be on the lookout and protect your Great Bombards from counterattack.

The Ottoman dynasty began with a mysterious vision. In the late 13th century, an Anatolian warlord named Osman visited the home of a well known Sufi mystic. That night, he dreamt that a moon rose from the holy man’s chest and sank into his own. Once it disappeared, Osman sprouted a massive tree from his navel, whose shade covered the world. Three centuries later, the empire bearing his name had grown to straddle three continents.

The Ottomans conscripted soldiers for their elite fighting force, the Janissary corps, from the Christian communities of western Anatolia and the Balkans. Recruiters brought adolescent boys from these regions to the imperial capital, converted them to Islam, and gave them combat and language training. The most promising trainees graduated to the palace service, and many of them eventually rose to the top of the Empire’s governing institutions.

During the 1453 siege of Constantinople, Mehmed the Conqueror employed a massive cannon, the Basilica, to demolish the city’s walled fortifications. Then the world’s largest artillery piece, it was capable of launching a 1,000-pound cannonball over a mile through the air. The Basilica could only be fired three times daily, and got so hot after each use that Ottoman soldiers had to douse it with olive oil to prevent it from cracking.

Congenitally mute royal attendants occupied a prestigious place at the Ottoman court, where silence was valued as a token of reverence. Palace mutes learned to communicate fluently with the Ottoman sultans using a complex form of sign language. Their unique access to the dynasty allowed them to acquire significant power and influence.

Before the Ottoman siege of Cairo in 1517, Selim I extended an offer of vassalage to his Mamluk counterpart, Tuman II, in exchange for the unconditional surrender of the city. Enraged by Selim’s condescension, Tuman sent back a withering rejection nailed to the decapitated head of a captured Ottoman soldier. Days later, Selim annihilated the rag-tag Mamluk army at the Battle of Raidaniyya, killing more than twenty-five thousand soldiers in a matter of hours.

From the lush coasts of West Africa to the dry sands of the Sahara, the medieval Malian Empire grew to become the largest and most prosperous empire in North Africa. Its incredible wealth in gold and salt, its stable trade routes, and its highly mobile army made its influence felt across the continents.
Leader must be downed before you can revive them

Owners of the power of speech, griots were musicians, genealogists, and councilors. They knew the stories of ancient ancestors and were feared because they could extort payment from people in public by shouting praises that turned to insults if not paid. Feared for their power to destroy reputations, in early times some were buried in the hollow trunks of baobab trees so their bodies would not pollute the earth.

When two armies faced each other before battle, the enemy commanders would meet in the middle of the field for a deadly ritual called sigifili. Each swore an oath boasting of his fearlessness and invincibility and snorted a pinch of poisonous snuff from his enemy’s pouch. If he staggered around and sneezed loudly without collapsing or dying, he had proven his worth as an enemy to be feared.

One weapon of sorcerers, who were often blacksmiths, was kòròtè, a tiny, possibly poisonous projectile that may or may not have been invisible. It forced the recipients to at least tell the truth, but could also sicken, cripple, or kill them. The kòròtè was directed and activated by incantations called kilisiw which were either benevolent or malevolent and were the basis of both positive and negative sorcery.

In Maninka and Bamana society, it is claimed, both jokingly and seriously, that it is the uncle’s duty to provide a wife for his nephew, the so-called "nephew wife." The obligation of this uncle-nephew bond is implied in the proverb: "When your uncle fails to give you a wife, he is no longer your uncle, he’s just your mother’s brother."
One Woman Worth a Thousand Others

Maminyan is the name of a legendary woman remembered as a great solver of women's problems. When a griot wants to honor a woman admired for her accomplishments, she is praised in a song as musolu ba maminyan, "the mother of all women who is ranked above all women" or wulu kelen maminyan, "Maminyan that is worth one thousand."

A trickster spirit, the wòkilò is a mischievous genie, forest sprite, or "little man" of the bush, said to be the size of an infant with feet pointing backwards. With a quiver slung over its shoulder and bow always strung, it lies in wait for unsuspecting people walking along the forest path and shoots arrows so tiny that they cannot be felt penetrating the flesh but cause serious injury.

In Age of Empires IV, you play as a civilization
Explore the world around you to locate resources which are essential for your civilization's progress
Use those resources to construct buildings, research new technologies and produce villagers and military units…
...all the while, fighting off a barrage of enemy raids and attacks
Guide your civilization through the ages and then, when the time is right, wage war on your enemies with the full might of your empire to claim victory
So, what are we waiting for?! Let's learn the basics of Age of Empires IV, through the eyes of the young Guillaume de Normandie, better known as William the Conqueror
The year is 1046 and Guillaume, the Duke of Normandy, is facing a rebellion of his nobles.
Guillaume’s cousin, Gui de Bourgogne, is leading a group of powerful lords in an attempt to claim Normandy for themselves.
During an attempt to seize him at Valognes, Guillaume narrowly escaped…
...and fled to the protection of his liege and strongest supporter, King Henri I of France.
After a year in King Henri’s court, Guillaume returned to Normandy with an army at his back and the burning desire to reclaim his dukedom…
Before Guillaume could join with his liege’s army, he needed to gather his own men and organize them into fighting order. To do this, he first needed to get a lay of the land
A collection of large rocks caught Guillaume’s eye. Wary of hidden threats, he decided to investigate.
Guillaume discovered a recently abandoned camp. A closer look would determine which direction the men went.
Happy knowing that his men were nearby, Guillaume refocused his attention.
Guillaume set off in search of his men, scouting the surroundings as he moved.
With each gallop of his horse’s hooves, more of Guillaume’s land was revealed to him.
Guillaume had found his spearmen. However they would need to be organized and prepared for battle. After which, he could continue searching for the rest of his forces.

With his spearmen found, Guillaume was one step closer to reclaiming his dukedom. First, he would need to organize them under his command, only then could he continue his search for the rest of his forces.
He gathered his spearmen and coordinated them into proper rank and file.
Guillaume knew that his men would need to follow his every command in the coming battle, and so he assigned them as a group under his control.
Guillaume’s spearmen were now a respectable fighting unit. It was time to march on in search of more reinforcements.
Guillaume marched on with his Spearmen, in search of more reinforcements to grow his army

Guillaume’s cousin and treacherous rebel, Gui de Bourgogne, had erected a wooden palisade along the path in an attempt to halt Guillaume’s progress. However, this was no problem for his spearmen who burned it down with ease.
An enemy patrol had been scouting the area. Guillaume knew that his spearmen were the perfect counter and saw this as a perfect opportunity to test their mettle.
Guillaume was a shrewd tactician. He knew that his spearmen were the perfect counter to the enemy horsemen
With his spearmen now proven in battle, Guillaume and his men moved on.

A group of enemy archers blocked Guillaume’s path. He knew their long range would give them a distinct advantage over his spearmen. As luck would have it, a regiment of his loyal horsemen had been spotted close by.
Guillaume once again proved he was a shrewd tactician. He knew his horsemen had the speed to run down and defeat the much slower archers.
With his horsemen now battle hardened, Guillaume focused on locating his own regiment of archers.

Guillaume found his archers hidden within a quiet village. They reported that a group of enemy spearmen were on their way to secure the area. Guillaume noticed the hill nearby and knew it would be a perfect spot for an ambush.
Guillaume’s plan once again proved true. His archers shot down the enemy Spearmen from their strategic position.
With his forces now gathered under his command, Guillaume prepared to rendezvous with his ally, King Henri I of France.
He gathered his forces and marched on
Now bolstered by his liege's forces, Guillaume marched to battle.

The time had finally come. Gui and his rebel army stood in open defiance against the combined forces of Guillaume and King Henri. Guillaume had mastered numerous skills and now it was time to put those skills to the test.
Henri had granted Guillaume the right to initiate the attack and, on his order, the fight for his dukedom would begin.
It was during this battle that Guillaume first demonstrated his prowess as a warrior of Viking descent. He single-handedly turned the tide of battle and inspired his men to victory.
With his forces depleted, Gui de Bourgogne was utterly defeated and fled. It was said that the dead were so numerous, they floated downstream and clogged the local waterwheel.

It was this victory at Val-ès-Dunes where Guillaume first displayed his militaristic genius and demonstrated his fighting prowess. It was here that he established himself as the undisputed Duke of Normandy.
1065, and Guillaume is marching west to deal with the last threat to his duchy, the Duke of Brittany, Conan II.
Guillaume was accompanied by an unusual ally, the powerful Anglo-Saxon Earl, Harold Godwinson.
Château de Dinan was a formidable fortress and would need an impressive army to siege its walls and bring down the keep.
Guillaume entrusted the raising of such an army to his wife, Mathilde de Flandre, Duchess of Normandy.
Mathilde knew that the key to a strong economy was a solid foundation laid by the most important of her subjects, the villagers.
First, Mathilde would need to gather resources, a job for her loyal villagers.
Resources were essential in constructing buildings and producing units, and could be found all across the land. But Mathilde knew the most important resource to begin gathering was food.
Food was key in recruiting villagers and military units.
Villagers could also construct a variety of military and economic buildings. Some of these buildings were used to make resource gathering more efficient, such as the mill.
Mathilde ordered construction of the mill close to the berry bushes as an alternative drop off point for her villagers.
Next was wood, a key resource in the construction of many buildings and essential to the success of any town. Mathilde constructed a lumber camp close to a forest, a good source of wood.
It was time for Mathilde to begin gathering the resources that ruled the known world: gold. Fortunately, a gold vein could be found nearby and Mathilde set her villagers to work mining it.
More villagers would be required if she was to raise a large army. To do this, she turned to her Town Center, an important landmark where new villagers could be recruited.
The town could no longer grow without additional homes. Mathilde ordered her villagers to construct new houses to accommodate the growing population.
With her food supply running low, Mathilde constructed farms to replace the depleted berry bushes. They would provide a constant supply of food.
Like all great leaders, Mathilde made expert use of local government. They aided her in organizing the villagers, and assigned them roles within the settlement.
With Conan's scouts defeated, Mathilde proved she was not to be trifled with. It wouldn’t be long before she could begin amassing a force large enough to bring Brittany to its knees.
Mathilde’s small town was now thriving and ready to progress to the next age of prosperity.
To mark the occasion and celebrate her villagers, Mathilde ordered the construction of a landmark.
Landmarks allowed a settlement to grow, granting access to more complex buildings and veteran units.
A new age had begun for the settlement, igniting a spark of inspiration and discovery.

The town had grown from just a handful of villagers to a fully established settlement. Mathilde had erected a landmark, announcing to the land that this town was a blossoming settlement. It was the perfect platform from which to forge an army and launch a siege on Conan’s castle.
It was time to build an army. Mathilde had everything she needed to amass a force capable of bringing down the walls of Dinan.
Mathilde was aware of Conan’s powerful horsemen. She knew spearmen would prove to be a useful counter. They would make up the core of her army.
Mathilde then turned her attention to producing her own horsemen. A particular strength of the Normans.

With the horsemen saddled and ready for war, Mathilde was confident they would be able to deal with any archer threat that may appear. Archers would be a useful tool to have in her own army, so Mathilde began the production of skilled bowmen.
But before the army could march upon Conan, Mathilde looked to bolster its might by conducting military research and improving their fighting prowess
Mathilde also commissioned that a Blacksmith be constructed, so that her army could be bestowed with the latest weaponry and armor.

They were met with a sprawling town and mighty army. Mathilde had done her job as duchess flawlessly, something Guillaume was very much used to. She would remain behind and efficiently run the settlement while Guillaume marched against Conan.
Guillaume knew this would not be an easy siege but was confident in his forces and his own proven abilities as a leader. Guillaume and Harold set off to the castle of Dinan.

This was the Château de Dinan, Conan’s stronghold in Brittany. With its capture, Brittany would never be in a position to threaten Normandy again. To capture it, Guillaume needed to seize Conan who had barricaded himself inside the keep atop the hill. He sounded the charge and laid siege to the keep.

Brittany was no longer a threat. Mathilde's administrative expertise had once again proved essential to Guillaume's strength. Harold was rewarded with arms, men and even a knighthood. According to Norman historians, it was here that Harold swore on holy relics to support Guillaume in his claim to the English throne. An oath sworn was a form of contract, recognized by law and church. Breaking one sworn upon a holy relic would be like breaking a contract with God and thus have dire consequences.

For now, it appeared that Guillaume had formed a strong alliance. However, it would not take long for it to lay in tatters. Guillaume and Harold would come to clash at one of the most infamous battles in history.
It is January 5th 1066 and Edward the Confessor, King of England, is dead.
Harold Godwinson, Earl of Wessex, is elected King of England the following day by the Witan. Harold also claimed that Edward named him his successor upon his deathbed.
For Guillaume, this was an absolute betrayal. He believed the throne of England was his by right.
Norman historians tell us Guillaume received consent for the invasion from Pope Alexander II, thus making a conquest of England a holy mission.

The port town of Saint-Valery-sur-Somme would be the location of Guillaume’s invasion preparations. He would build up his economy, gather an army and sail the channel to begin his conquest of England and take revenge on the now King Harold
In order to amass an invasion force capable of taking the English throne, Guillaume would once again need to establish a strong economy.
He set about converting this small port town into an economic stronghold

Norman scouts returning from England informed Guillaume that the Saxon army was positioned along the South Coast. Guillaume decided to wait until he could make an unopposed landing. He thus continued his preparations, turning his attention to constructing the key buildings needed for his army.
Although his resources were still plenty, Guillaume would need more if he was to raise a capable force. Constructing a Market would allow him to barter for further resources.

With the key infrastructure now constructed, and his resources stockpiled, Guillaume turned his attention to raising his army. He would need a huge invasion force to take on the full might of England.

He needed to ferry a massive army, with their supplies, armor, weapons and horses across the body of water now known as the English Channel. He began construction of a dockyard capable of building him his fleet.
With his fleet now built, Guillaume set about the momentous task of loading his forces.

With the winds now more favorable and reports of Harold’s army marching north to fight off a Viking threat, Guillaume was finally ready to launch his invasion. He plotted his course and set sail for England.

Guillaume would land at the old Roman fort of Pevensey. The Anglo-Saxons had taken up residence inside but they would prove no match for Guillaume’s trained soldiers. He landed his forces and took the settlement.

With Pevensey now under his control, Guillaume took the time to rest before marching onto the nearby town of Hastings. From there he would resupply his forces and wait for word of Harold and his army.

Guillaume now had a foothold in England. From here, he would have time to secure his location and resupply his forces before marching north and meeting Harold in battle. It would be at Senlac Hill that Guillaume, Duke of Normandy, would come to be forever known as William the Conqueror.

In the name of God, the Compassionate, the Merciful...
In unreckoned number, horse and foot, lord and commoner alike, they crossed the threshold to the east with a lust for war and plunder.
Their ambition: to seize the holy city of Jerusalem... and all Bilad al-Sham beyond.
The infidels prevailed, but their lawless conquest would be answered... by the roar of our righteous lions!
These pages tell the story of our struggle, of the courage of great leaders who fought for our land and our faith.
With God's favor upon them, they unsheathed their swords and sharpened their spears, and earned their place in our eternal memory.
I begin this story at the last bastion of the faithful, the ancient fortress of Tyre – may God protect her.
Much of Bilad al-Sham fell to the invaders.
For many years, our rulers did little to turn fortune to our favor.
Some fought against their brethren, seeking petty advantages, and even joining with the enemy.
God sent us three worthy leaders whose boldness restored courage and hope to the faithful.
God sent us worthy leaders whose boldness restored courage and hope to the faithful.
First came Zangi, who, with the fury of wildfire, seized the city of Edessa.
Zangi. With the fury of wildfire, he seized the city of Edessa.
Next came Zangi's son, Nur al-Din, who called for unity among the Muslim forces, and all hearts were attached to his cause.
Nur al-Din, who called for unity among the Muslim forces, and all hearts were attached to his cause.
Boldest of them all was Nur al-Din's student, Salah al-Din Yusuf – whom the Franks called Saladin.
Boldest of them all was Salah al-Din Yusuf – whom the Franks called Saladin.
As a young man, Saladin showed great promise.
Blessed with a mind sharp and bright as a Damascene blade, he would one day prove himself a skilled tactician.
But like all on the path to greatness, he would first be tested in the forge-fire of war.
The vizier of Egypt, a scheming vulture named Shawar, faced a fierce rival for his office.
As his enemy drew near, Shawar came to Nur al-Din, offering handsome payment for the removal of this rival.
And so, the glorious army of Damascus marched into the vulture's realm.
The vizier's rival was indeed put to the sword... but Shawar refused to pay what he had promised.
Trembling before Nur al-Din's retribution, Shawar joined ranks with the accursed king of Jerusalem – our shared enemy!
But his betrayal sealed his doom, for the Franks coveted Egypt for themselves.
In the long and anguished war for Egypt, Saladin would rise.
Vying against Christian and Muslim rulers alike, Saladin in time rose to power.
But a dreadful turmoil bedeviled his realm.
A demon disguised as a man, one Reynald of Châtillon, preyed on Muslim merchants and pilgrims.
With all the folly of a man deranged, Reynald dared gnash at the heart of Islam itself.
Saladin would hunt this dishonorable wretch over land and sea.
Saladin's eye was fixed on Jerusalem.
He – 'The Righteousness of the Faith' – desired nothing more than to bring the Aqsa sanctuary back into the fold of the faithful.
At this time, the demon Reynald broke yet another treaty, wickedly seizing a caravan of Muslim goods and traders.
Saladin swore an oath to slay this monster by his own hand.
Calling to arms every man, sword, and bow under his banner, Saladin made ready to keep his vow.
In rushing to their doom at Hattin, the Franks had emptied out the great garrisons of the occupied cities.
Their gates were unguarded and their people without a blade to defend them.
Trembling before Saladin's eminence, they submitted:
Beirut, Acre, Ascalon, Bethlehem...until the victorious day came when the sultan entered Jerusalem itself.
But the sacred city would yet see much bloodstained struggle for her hand.
More than sixty years passed in this way.
The Franks again renewed their quest for Jerusalem.
This time they believed – in their unending folly – that Egypt was the path to their glory.
Had they not learned from their misfortune!?
Did they think our roars were mere whispers on the wind!?
The commander of the army, Baybars al-Bunduqdari, assembled a host of mamluk warriors so great that the whole earth shook beneath their step.
At the ailing sultan's side, his beloved wife, Shajar al-Durr – our Tree of Pearls – ably took charge in his stead.
With cleverness and caution, she directed Egypt's defense.
The Franks would swing their swords at shadows, stumbling with all confidence ever deeper into the lions' den.
A new power rose under the banner of former slaves.
The empire of the Mamluks was born.
The ravages of the Franks – monstrous though they were – could not match the sudden terror brought upon us by the thunder and malice of the Mongols.
Like the unstoppable spread of a plague, they were upon us.
Our lands were choked with smoke and ran with blood.
But Sultan Qutuz – may God be merciful to him – would not stand idle nor flee in fear.
He gathered his Mamluk army... and marched.
The Mongol scourge harried our lands again and again.
Each new attack we repelled with great vigor, our spirits emboldened, and our arrows guided by God's hand.
But the heart of the Sultan Qalawun was deeply anguished, knowing the Mongols might join with the Franks and crush the lands of Islam from east and west.
More vexing still, the Franks possessed the port of Acre – an open door to any new infidel army.
The hour had come to close all such doors; to rid Bilad al-Sham of every last fortress of the unbelievers.
The Mamluk empire was strong as cedar... though its rulers came and went like the winds of the Middle Sea.
The changing breeze calmed for a time when Sultan al-Ashraf Barsbay came to rule, and prosperity settled across our lands.
But a resting lion attracts biting flies.
The ancient blight of piracy plagued us at sail and ashore.
The accursed King Janus of Cyprus granted warm welcome to wicked men seeking riches and slaves.
These thieves and killers assaulted our towns and our people, carrying off in great number whatever they pleased – may God curse them.
Barsbay gathered a great invasion fleet... a fleet so terrible as to fill the entire horizon.
There would be no forgiveness for the wicked.
The sultan's fleet returned, victorious, with a fortunate few of our stolen families...
...and the wicked Frankish king in chains.
Some say he was paraded through the jeering crowds of Cairo, shackled and barefoot, riding on a lame donkey.
He kissed the ground at the sultan's feet, they say.
Others tell a more dignified tale: the sultan greeted the king as a guest and imprisoned him in comfort.
Be he cruel or benevolent, Sultan Barsbay has sealed the dominance of Egypt over this incessant pest.
My story ends today, yet our story will go on.
Just as the tales of our great heroes are retold in these pages, so our triumphs yet to come will be added to their legacy, in sha'a Allah.
Just as the tales of our great heroes are retold in these pages, so our triumphs yet to come will be added to their legacy, God willing.
I pray this blessed peace will last the rest of our days.
