The First Crusade of the Franks claimed much of the Holy Land. But one city had yet eluded its grasp.
In the decade-long struggle for Egypt, an ambitious young Saladin would come to the fore.
A hostile Frankish fleet stirred the peaceful waters of the Red Sea. They would soon feel the fury of Saladin's navy.
Under Louis IX, the Franks stirred the sands of Egypt in their renewed quest for Jerusalem.
A new menace threatened the sultanate itself: the Mongols had arrived.
Amid the ashes of broken treaties, the Mamluks would burn the last Frankish port to the ground.
Pirates and raiders tormented the Egyptian coast. They would soon feel the fire of Mamluk retribution.
Saladin's hour had come. Gathering every warrior to his banner, he set his greatest battleplan in motion.

As refugees sought haven within its walls, Tyre prepared to face a Frankish war machine of unprecedented scale.
The King of Jerusalem sought control over Egypt and began taking territory. But Nur al-Din's army would battle him for dominance with two shrewd leaders at the helm.
Led by the reckless Reynald of Châtillon, the Franks brought their marauding to the Muslims of the Red Sea. But their depredations would not go unanswered.
Seizing on decades of animosity, Saladin harnessed the fury of the Muslim armies against the Franks. He planned to lure the enemy in haste to Tiberias.
A new army of Franks attacked under King Louis IX, determined to seize Egypt as a gateway to Jerusalem.
With Baybars at his side, Sultan Qutuz marched to intercept the dreaded Mongols.
On the eve of battle, Sultan Qalawun died, passing the sultanate to his son, Al-Ashraf Khalil. With Damascus's mightiest weapons, he took up his father's mission to expel the Franks.
The Mediterranean Sea was infested with pirates given haven on Cyprus. Incensed by their raiding, the Mamluks raised an invasion fleet.
The alliance forged by Tyre and Damascus ruined the efforts of the Franks on this day. The victory was small in scale, but mighty in spirit.
The arduous contest for Egypt was over, Nur al-Din taking the spoils. Saladin rose to the office of Vizier, marking the beginnings of his steady ascent to power.
The Ayyubid fleet outmatched and scattered Reynald's belligerent raiders. The Red Sea and its people were secure.
Saladin's sword was steady and true; his oath to slay Reynald was fulfilled. At last, Saladin's path to Jerusalem lay open before him.
The Franks turned tail and aborted their ill-fated quest. The mighty Mamluks had felled the once-feared enemy and now prepared to grasp power for themselves.
The Mamluks shook the very core of the Mongol invasion, and the invincible enemy was at last defeated.
The Mamluks' mighty trebuchets crushed the last significant Frankish port in the east. Defeated, the Franks sailed away, never again to stake a claim on the Holy Land.
The Mamluks razed the Cypriot capital, took its king, and freed what captives they could. Subdued and disgraced, Cyprus would now pay tribute to the Mamluks.

"We chroniclers have been preserving the deeds of our people for centuries. We toil by dim candlelight, illuminating our stories for the world, but rarely are we seen.

Witnesses to history, protectors of our truth, we gather these accounts at times with blade in one hand and pen in the other.

Take Abu al-Fida', who bravely fought off the Franks alongside his sultan, al-Ashraf Khalil. God saw him to victory and he repaid this blessing by devoting himself to scholarship.

He and many others have carried our voices forward with honor.

Were it not for such dedication, I would have no story to tell you today."

~ The Chronicler

"The Franks were brazen in their belief that they could conquer us. But their boldness would be cut down by our blades of Damascus steel.

Those infidels who fled with their lives carried legendary tales of our swords westward. They told of metal that bent with the wind but was so sharp as to split a grain of sand.

Our steel was forged in fire but bore the signature of the seas – the power of ocean waves patterned into the blade itself.

This distinction, the Franks came to fear. When our superior swords sang, their spirits were silenced.

Such blades were reserved for our elite warriors and the craft to our trusted smiths, so that our great secret would never be stolen."

~The Chronicler

"The Franks descended upon the Red Sea, seeking only destruction; never before had our pilgrims faced such wickedness.

The most gallant commander of the Ayyubid fleet, Husam al-Din Lu'lu', would make his vengeance felt like the bellow of a wrathful lion.

Shrewdly, he gathered our most hardened sailors and bent his course upon the intruders. Without hesitation, he cut down or captured every infidel man and ship, returning to the people that which had been stolen.

Lu'lu' then set upon the demon Reynald himself.

At the crucial moment, Reynald found his crew of cowards no match for our superior seamanship.

His evil attack was thwarted by Lu'lu''s steadfast valor, and Saladin ordered the prisoners be put to the blade for their villainy." 

~ The Chronicler

"Saladin's trusted general, Gökböri, known as the 'Blue Wolf', was highly favored for his loyalty and his deeds.

Firstborn of the Lord of Erbil, Gökböri stood to inherit his father's title. But he was betrayed by his own overlord, the Atabeg of Mosul, who thought him unfit for rule. His birthright denied, he fled into Saladin's service, acting to unite the people beneath him and earning the sultan's favor.

But it was at the Battle of Hattin that Gökböri's honor would be proven. While Saladin's forces were routed, the Blue Wolf bravely stood alone against the Frankish charge. His ferocity inspired our warriors back to the battlefield, and our victory was secured.

Saladin's reward to his fearsome general: Erbil.

Gökböri retired comfortably to his home, heralded as a hero. He lived out the remainder of his life in service to his people, undefeated, and beloved by all."

~ The Chronicler

"A former concubine, Sultana Shajar al-Durr had many powerful minds set against her. They would not allow a woman to rule.

Her wisdom, however, would see her name remembered and theirs forgotten.

Reigning for only 80 days, she wasted no time in building architectural marvels that would decorate Cairo for centuries to come.

Overseeing her own mausoleum's construction, she boldly broke with tradition by commissioning a mosaic of a tree of pearls, her namesake.

This piece artfully evaded the ban on depictions of people within houses of worship, and did so brazenly – she placed it inside the most holy space in the building, the mihrab.

Through her patronage, Shajar al-Durr secured her everlasting legacy, and defied those that sought to erase her."

~The Chronicler

"Our magnificent Arab horses are as formidable as the warriors who ride them into battle.

Forged in the heat of the desert sun, and hardened by the freezing nights, these beautiful beasts are the envy of all.

Hearty enough to ride long distances without food or drink, they possess both stamina and speed; they are the perfect mounts for our fearsome riders.

When our stallions lift their heads, breath fills their lungs with such force that the Franks can hear their end descending upon them.

For many, the sound of hooves hard as steel hammering on the desert bedrock is the last they ever hear."

~ The Chronicler

"Looking down on Acre as its walls crumbled to his overpowering siege, Sultan al-Ashraf Khalil heard the entreaty of King Henry's envoys, sent to sue for peace.

The Sultan goaded the Frankish messengers, asking if they had brought him the keys to the city.

At that moment, an immense boulder crashed to the earth beside him. 

Thrown by a Frankish catapult from within Acre, this single stone had defiled the truce the Franks themselves had called.

Enraged by this apparent betrayal, Sultan al-Ashraf Khalil renewed the attack that his father, Qalawun, had started.

All of Acre would burn for this offense, from the towers of the shining city to every last man within."

~ The Chronicler

"So quickly, before Barsbay, were our leaders replaced. So hopeless became our dream of an enduring Mamluk fleet.

Shipbuilding was costly and slow; thus, our sultans favored other methods of thwarting enemy landings: we burned our own ports to the ground.

In foolishly reducing our influence over the Middle Sea, we left our people of the coast defenseless. They, more than any, bore the hardships of Frankish villainy. 

Barsbay would not stand for this wickedness.

Hurriedly, he raised a fleet so great that no single harbor could moor it. 

Upon their return from victory at Cyprus, the ships, by necessity, dispersed and anchored their mighty number off many a coastal town.

Never again shall we wither at the sight of foreign sails on the horizon."

~ The Chronicler

Town Centers can be upgraded into special buildings called the Daimyo Manor, Daimyo Palace, and Shogunate Castle. These buildings provide bonuses to nearby Villagers working Farms by %1%%%, %2%%%, %3%%%, increases the capacity of Samurai Bannerman by +%4%, +%5%, +%6%, and improves its defenses.

• Samurai: Man-at-Arms replacement. Heavy melee infantry that have Deflective Armor which can block melee and ranged attacks.

• Mounted Samurai: Lancer replacement. Heavy melee cavalry with Deflective Armor.

• Yumi Ashigaru: Archer with improved move speed for reduced cost and health.

• Onna-Bugeisha: Light melee infantry that can use their fast move speed and long weapon range to keep their opponent on alert.

• Onna-Musha: Crossbowman replacement. Cavalry Archer that can use increased mobility to harass heavy targets.

• Shinobi: Espionage unit that can act as a spy, sabotage buildings, or assassinate targets. Produced from the Koka Township Landmark.

• Ozutsu: Heavy ranged gunpowder infantry that has a huge handheld cannon with high siege and splash damage. Produced from the Tanegashima Gunsmith Landmark.

The port of Tyre had yet held out against capture.
But in this year, the King of Jerusalem attacked with renewed fervor.
Foreseeing the city's demise, Tyre's governor beseeched the ruler of Damascus to hasten a vigorous force to his aid.
At great speed, the most honorable Tughtekin drew up to the city with a host of his Turkic horse archers.
With cunning on their side, the Damascenes set to attacking the Franks.
Their hooves thundered and their arrows flew.
They struck with such speed that the enemy would never know his own assailant.
The Franks, at this time, bowed to their King Baldwin of Jerusalem, a man whose rigid heart was fixed on the conquest of Tyre.
To this end, Baldwin's men began constructing a colossal siege weapon – a wheeled beast, a ram of thunderous power, a destroyer of cities.
The Damascenes had little time to gather their strength before the titan attacked.
Relentlessly, the Franks did the bidding of their king, toiling by sun and moon to build his war machine.
Though the stout city walls had borne Tyre through countless attacks, they now faced a trial of unimagined scale.
Within the city, every archer, craftsman, smith, and sailor were called to the walls in Tyre’s most desperate hour.
Fertile fishing waters lay close to the shore.
The Damascenes had only to command a few of their number to fish there, and no soldier would need endure the roar of an empty belly.
The Franks concluded the construction of their infernal colossus.
They filled the belly of this beast with rank upon rank of infidel cowards.
They meant to traverse the land bridge within the juggernaut, only joining the battle should the beast fall.
As the beast labored along its forsaken path, the Damascenes raised their battle-cry, united in their zeal to slay this monster.
The wicked tower erupted in splinter and flame.
Its accursed architects fled, tail-turned and ashamed in their failure.
Tyre, city of the ancients, gateway to the Middle Sea, and now fortress of refuge, rejoiced in its hard-fought victory.
The Tyrians wept and thanked God for His fearless Damascene deliverers.
Into Egypt marched Nur al-Din’s army of Damascus.
At its head was Asad al-Din Shirkuh – "The Lion of the Mountain" – charged with settling the turmoil that festered among the rulers of the Fatimids.
The Franks, too, staked their claim on Egypt, intruding again and again with unquenchable greed.
In these years of ever-changing alliances, the army of Damascus was bent on one enduring end: the expulsion of the infidels.
The Franks had staked their claim on Egypt, intruding again and again with unquenchable greed.
Meanwhile, Egypt's Fatimid rulers fought one another for power, their struggles spilling over into Nur al-Din's realm.
And so, into Egypt marched the army of Damascus, with Asad al-Din Shirkuh at its head, charged with settling the festering turmoil and expelling the Franks.
Shirkuh’s young nephew, Saladin, would hold the port of Alexandria while the army strove to repel the Frankish advance.
Though Nur al-Din’s mind was not yet fixed on rule over Egypt, it was of great import that his army break the muster of the Franks.
Asad al-Din Shirkuh, a military man of Kurdish origin, served at Nur al-Din's behest.
Asad al-Din Shirkuh – "The Lion of the Mountain" – a military man of Kurdish origin, served at Nur al-Din's behest.
He led his men into the mire of Egypt's strife with unwavering courage.
Salah al-Din Yusuf – called Saladin by the Franks – was at this time a young man, unproven in battle but cunning as a spider.
King Amalric of Jerusalem ruled the Franks at this time, his ambition spreading its tendrils far beyond the holy city.
He sought to bring Cairo under his sway, and thus set about the long battle for territory.
Fatimid Egypt churned with political unrest.
Some made pacts with foes of any banner, only to change their allegiance when fortunes shifted.
Shirkuh could add local men to his numbers by liberating their territory from the Franks.
For each territory captured, Shirkuh would gain the support of the local Fatimid garrison.
Together they would wrest control from the Franks.
Shirkuh knew that the Franks would not stand idle as their losses mounted.
He would rally his men to defend the newly gained territories.
The city-port of Alexandria, gateway to the Nile delta, was a critical location.
Here, fresh Muslim troops would disembark and join the fight.
Saladin’s great defensive strength would be needed to guard the port against capture.
With the wind at their backs, reinforcements arrived at the port with a fresh zeal for the fight.
More men would be delivered to the port – so long as the Franks did not bring it to ruin.
Fresh warriors from Damascus stood ready at Alexandria, awaiting their hour to slay the enemy.
As they mustered, the men sharpened their blades and steeled their spirits.
The conflict wound unendingly as the Nile itself.
Seeking the advantage, each side stoked the fires of war as ever more soldiers rallied to the front.
The port lay unguarded as the Franks made a thunderous approach.
The struggle would come to naught if the Franks laid waste to Alexandria.
The forsaken toil of the Franks bore them ever closer to victory.
With the warmth of dawn upon them, the Damascenes seized the upper hand in the battle for control.
With God’s favor upon them, the Damascenes seized the upper hand in the battle for control.
Reduced in number and spirit, the Franks of Jerusalem broke their camp and began their joyless march out of Egypt.
Shirkuh soon claimed Cairo for Nur al-Din, but Egypt's governance would not long after fall to his illustrious nephew, Saladin.

The once prosperous waters of the Red Sea were now impoverished by the plundering of Reynald of Châtillon.
But the spirited Muslim fleet would fix every bow and ballista on expelling this unruly malice.
Of greatest import was the protection of Muslim trade ships whose exertions sustained the prosperity of the local people and the Ayyubid Sultanate.
If trade floundered, so too would Saladin’s hold on the Red Sea.
The coastal towns bustled with trade, their livelihoods dependent on safe passage over the seas.
In the face of Frankish intrusion, the Ayyubids would gather a great swell of wealth to defend their waters and see off the enemy.
In this year, Saladin had ruled in his own name some nine years.
Chroniclers would name his dynasty after his father, Ayyub.
The Franks struck down the trade ships without hesitation.
Though many vessels were lost to the depths, the trade route could still be saved.
Unrest grew with each lost ship.
Some Muslims loaded their beasts and fled to safer towns, leaving fewer behind to engage in trade.
Time was against the fleet. It would protect the Red Sea at all cost, lest the region fell into destitution.
The Frankish terror grew so great that Muslim trade dwindled to a trickle.
The Ayyubid fleet had little time to settle the unrest.
To the east lay an island haven, full of promise... but infested with lawless pirates.
Hidden in the mist lay an island haven, full of promise... but infested with lawless pirates.
Many treasures could be uncovered there if the fleet was sharp of mind and bold of spirit.
Not content to pierce our flesh by arrow and blade, the Franks unleashed such vessels of ruin as the Red Sea had never known.
The Franks, seeing our sails ever filled with the breath of the wind, redoubled their effort to break our muster.
Launching the flagship of Reynald, they bore great ranks of barbarians to our shore, the better to stifle our enterprise.
The skill of the Ayyubid sailors prevailed.
Reynald’s fleet abandoned their evil-doing and left the Red Sea, never to blight these waters again.
On the eve of battle, Saladin laid a trap for Reynald and the Franks.
He ordered the citadel of Tiberias besieged – not for conquest, but to lure.
A countess dwelt within, and the Frankish lords were sure to charge to her rescue.
It is said that, in spite of our great numbers, Reynald himself led the charge, saying: "The fire is not daunted by the quantity of wood to burn."
How he would be mocked by the echo of his own words!
The Franks had marched a great distance through arid lands without rest or water.
Now they would choke and stumble through fields set aflame by Saladin’s vanguard.
Saladin planned to use the land itself to defeat the Frankish army.
He would seek out the most advantageous battleground and corral the exhausted knights, so bringing them to their end.
As they neared the battle, the Franks took frequent stops to rest their horses and gather their numbers.
Filled with a lust for the fight, some of Saladin’s men wildly attacked Tiberias – unbidden by their sultan.
Saladin admonished them to contain their zeal and direct their energies to the battle with the Franks.
Joining the battle, the infamous Knights Templar rode headlong into the valley.
This order of lords and priests fought under their cross, but their zeal would be no match for the warriors of Islam.
Now entered into the fray the Knights of the Order of Saint John, known to their brethren as the Hospitallers.
Among the Franks, the Hospitallers were healers of great renown.
Their self-named "Grand Master" would lend his skill to the repair and invigoration of his weary knights.
Proud Reynald himself, aflush with bloodlust, charged now onto the field, the theatre of his own undoing.
In a cloud of arrows and smoke, the battle was won.
The utter ruin of the knights was so complete that almost none survived.
At last, Saladin fulfilled his long-held oath, striking down Reynald with one true stroke of his sword.
They say that Saladin knelt before God and wept tears of joy, giving thanks for this most glorious of victories.

It was written that every place the Mongols attacked they captured, and every army they fought they defeated.
Such was the anguish of the people.
But the Mamluks would not be so easily swept aside.
Like many Muslim armies before them, they would use the land itself to set their trap.
Baybars, the hero of Mansurah, commanded his loyal horse archers to lure the Mongols into the Mamluk snare.
Baybars, the hero of Mansurah, commanded his cavalry to lure the Mongols into the Mamluk snare.
The first Mongol riders drove into the valley.
Their numbers were small and their khan absent, leaving them undisciplined and unwary.
The Mamluks slew the first host of Mongols as swiftly as gourds on the training field.
But more would soon swarm into the valley.
Then arrived the vast numbers of the Mongol invasion force.
And so began the test of Baybars' trap.
In this year, some Franks forged an accursed alliance with the Mongols – may God abandon them.
But the Mamluks would not recoil from the faithless shayatin, no matter their banner or lord.
But the Mamluks would not recoil from the faithless beasts, no matter their banner or lord.
Some chroniclers tell us that amid the fighting, the Mamluks unleashed a powerful new weapon: the handcannon.
They blasted the Mongols and startled their horses, it is said, which put the invaders in a blind panic.
Sultan Sayf al-Din Qutuz – "The Sword of the Faith" – rallied his warriors and inspired them to keep up the attack.
Now joining with his men, the Mongol general, Kitbuqa, came into the valley.
His presence seemed to invigorate his followers and rile them into a feverish new assault.
Our victory that day changed the story of the Mongol invasion.
Qutuz and Baybars made them taste the cup of death and all hearts were gladdened by the news.
Never again would we shrink at the sound of the Mongol war drums.
Acre was a fortress like no other.
Her fortuitous location had made her a desirable conquest for countless peoples since the dawn of civilization itself.
She had endured much, and her walls were all the stronger for it.
On this day, the last Franks of the Kingdom of Jerusalem would cower behind Acre’s walls for as long as they would hold.
The Mamluks drew up their mightiest siege machines – two titans of war they named "The Furious" and "The Victorious."
With the roar of these colossal beasts, they would reduce the fortress to rubble, leaving no hope of return for the invaders.
The great city had been remade by many conquerors: Romans and Greeks, Persians and Arabs, and for the last hundred years, the Franks.
The Knights Templar, Hospitaller, and Teutonic each kept their strongholds within Acre’s walls.
The great stones of the Mamluk behemoths shattered their targets, rousing the Franks to counterattack.
Sallying from the fortress, the Franks would strike at the trebuchets themselves... but leave their posts unmanned.
The waters of the Middle Sea surrounded Acre on three sides, every wave now churning with enemy vessels hastening to the aid of the Franks.
Acre’s harbor was a considerable menace to the Mamluk camp.
Attack vessels spewed from its mouth, bound for a flanking assault on the exposed shore of the siege operation.
Flame and dust consumed the ruined city.
The last Frankish king to occupy Bilad al-Sham sailed in haste for his safe haven of Cyprus.
With unmatched valor, the Mamluks had written the last chapter in two centuries of Frankish intrusion.
The accursed crusade of the infidels had come to its inevitable end.
The great Mamluk fleet bore down on the island of Cyprus.
With rage in their hearts, Barsbay's men would bring ruin upon this harbor of evil-doers.
The infernal vessels of the Franks snapped at our ships like empty-bellied crocodiles.
We could not allow them control of the waters!
Like the unending tides they assailed us, wave after wave.
But cunning was on our side.
The Mamluks would fix their aim at the bedrock of Frankish power – at their lumber, their farms, and their gold.
Many captive Muslims were brought to this accursed place to toil under whip and chain.
Among them, my habiba... and my son.
Among them, my beloved... and my son.
At last, the Mamluks could liberate our people, and avenge this most shameful villainy.
As our presence on the island took firm root, so our passage over the waters grew bolder.
The sultan saw the fruit of our determination and ordered a flotilla of trade ships be anchored offshore.
The sultan saw the fruit of our determination and ordered a trade outpost be anchored offshore.
Shaken by the boldness of the Mamluk fleet, the Franks attacked even our civilian trade ships.
At this time, the port of Famagusta was operated by the Italian merchants of Genoa.
Genoa lent its mighty navy and sturdy crossbowmen to the cause of the Franks.
Nicosia was a proud and stout capital with a garrison of Franks as fierce as they were foolish.
Razing the city would be a feat worthy of glory, but a task that could so easily flounder.
Before launching their final assault, the Mamluks would ensure that their strength was unmatched, and that the allies of the Franks were expunged.
The Mamluks marked their triumph in rubble and ash.
Our wrath left Cyprus in ruin and without its king.
Frankish ears would roar with the sound of their defeat with every crashing wave of the Middle Sea.
The sultanate of the Mamluks was born.
The Mamluk realm was strong as cedar... though its rulers came and went like the winds of the Middle Sea.
The Hardest A.I. with a bonus to resource gather rates.
The Hardest A.I. with a large bonus to resource gather rates.
The Hardest A.I. with a massive bonus to resource gather rates.
Making camp in the Nile delta, the French king, Louis IX, readied – some say – fifty thousand men for his attack on Egypt.
But numbers alone would not discourage the valiant hearts of Egypt’s defenders.
The sultan, now on the threshold of death, conferred all power to his wife, Shajar al-Durr.
She would exploit the arrogance of the enemy with her own unmatched cunning.
Shajar al-Durr, the beloved wife of the sultan, had long observed the mechanisms of governance and war.
Her trusted informants would now deliver the sultanate from the brink of conquest.
Shajar al-Durr's messengers sped like the wind, carrying her request to the army of Baybars...
"Hurry to battle. Your hour has come."
The invaders were yet undeterred by the humiliations of their past, suffered at our hands.
With the blessing of their Pope, the Franks embarked with all speed once again on the path to calamity.
Alongside the ranks of the Templars and Hospitallers were the German knights of the Teutonic Order.
They were stout and sturdy, and wielded the crude blades of the savages.
The unruly legions were accompanied on this doomed expedition by their infidel holy men.
These Grand Masters seemed to bestow an ungodly vigor on the men whom they bound to their spell.
Only when the Grand Masters were sent to the grave and their camp destroyed could the Muslims rest.
The breaking of dawn heralded the arrival of Baybars and his Mamluk horse lords.
The Mamluks came from the Turkic lands in Central Asia, bringing with them the wild spirit and terrifying skill of battle-scarred steppe warriors.
Baybars al-Bunduqdari, himself a Turk, had risen from slave soldier to commander of the Mamluk forces.
He was now poised to prove the worth of his command and his people.
With their commanders captured or slain, those Franks still breathing abandoned their assault.
From this moment, the nearby city would be named in honor of our triumph: al-Mansurah, "The Victorious."

He planned to undermine the siege by attacking the Franks' resources.
Without hesitation, Tughtekin destroyed the Frankish camps.
The more he did so, the more he weakened the attack on Tyre.
Warriors and refugees arrived by land and sea, eager to join the fight.
The nearby settlement would serve as a place to muster a fearsome army.
Word came that the Franks had gathered a great stockpile of lumber.
They surely intended the wood for their siege weaponry.
Tughtekin would rally every spear and horseman to bring their torches to battle.
The Frankish garrison buzzed with the drone of a thousand hornets, ready to swarm.
The Damascenes would surely perish in such a nest of malevolence.
Deploying every skill at their disposal, the Muslims sent trade ships to the city to keep the people supplied and the coin flowing.
Ferocious as it seemed, the beast's weakness was its wooden construction.
With enough flame-bearers the Damascenes could burn it to ash.
Tughtekin called on his spearmen and his horsemen to ready their torches.
They filled the belly of this beast with rank upon rank of the king's cowards.
They meant to traverse the land bridge within the juggernaut, only joining the battle should it come under attack.
Shirkuh’s young nephew, Saladin, was charged with holding the port of Alexandria while the army strove to repel the Frankish advance.
Joining their Frankish brethren, a new foe emerged onto the field: the Knights Templar.
This order of lords and priests fought under their cross, bolstered by the blessing of their pope.
Red Sea ship-builders quickly set to bolstering the fleet.
Vessels of all kinds would be needed to put down the Frankish raids.
Claiming the island for themselves, the Ayyubids collected the spoils.
The Ayyubid treasury burgeoned with coin.
The tireless industry of the fleet held sway over the troubled seas.
They launched the flagship of Reynald.
With their last gasp, the Franks bore great ranks of barbarians to the harbors of our towns, the better to stifle our enterprise.
The dry grass burned only for a short time.
Once the flames had died, they could not be rekindled.
The fires would have to be lit only when the Franks were nearby.
The enemy's breath was blackened with soot and his luster diminished with every ill-fated step.
The breathless Franks fell, but more would soon replace them.
Word came that the main body of the Frankish army had begun its approach.
The many roads to Tiberias forked widely across the hills.
Saladin's men had much ground to cover.
He ordered his troops be ready along all paths the enemy might take.
The Templars were emboldened by the zeal of their leader, the self-named "Grand Master."
Their Grand Master would lend his skill to the repair and invigoration of his weary knights.
The enemy neared Tiberias, crossing the threshold of the great mounds known as the Horns of Hattin.
They say that Saladin wept tears of joy, giving thanks for this most glorious of victories.
The Grand Master of the Hospitallers went to his grave, and his followers departed the delta.
The hardy leader of the Teutons breathed his last at our hands, and his men withdrew.
The exalted Templar Grand Master – at the end made only of flesh – fell to our swords, and his knights fled our pursuit.
Before day's end, the Mongols would know the meaning of defeat.
The Mongol riders drove into the valley, heedless of the ruin that awaited them.
Strong in number and firm of spirit, the multitudes of the Mamluk army joined the battle.
The enemy was upon them and defeat loomed near.
Qutuz would now face the Mongol general, Kitbuqa.
Kitbuqa led his horde in their final desperate charge.
With the keep now in sight, the Mamluks could call down a great barrage of stone from their mighty trebuchets.
Mamluk siege engineers raised the alarm.
Their great trebuchets had suffered much damage at the hands of the Franks.
Should the machines fail, so too would the Mamluks' hope of ousting the enemy.
Ever deeper into the fortress they pushed, leaving few corners of refuge for the desperate Franks.
The first target of the Mamluks' retribution was the town of Limassol.
With the town in hand, the Mamluks could redouble their efforts, asserting their presence on the island and in the waters.
Cries of joy rose from the camp as the captives were freed.
The Franks would feel the loss of those resources gathered here, which now bolstered the Mamluks' advance.
Our ships came under attack from the Italian navy of Genoa, which emboldened the cause of the Franks.
Genoa harbored its vessels at the port of Famagusta.
From here, the Genoese launched ceaseless attacks on our fleet.
Only by destroying their docks could the Mamluk vessels control the seas.
The invasion was strengthened by the ingress of our trade ships.
But the port of Famagusta yet posed a great threat to their passage.
From the king's capital of Nicosia poured scores of his guard, with orders to stop our advance by any measure.
The town of Kyrenia harbored Frankish vassals.
They would answer the king's call to battle should the Mamluks enter the walls of the capital.

Smoke is a powerful tactic against larger groups of enemies. They will be weakened and easier to defeat.

The House of Wisdom is the only Landmark available to your civilization. Add Wings to it to Age Up.

Jeanne d'Arc: A hero unit that starts as a villager, Jeanne begins the Journey of a Hero to gain powerful abilities.

Jeanne's Rider: Fast Cavalry effective at raiding, flanking, and countering Crossbows, becomes available once Jeanne achieves level 3.

A spark of hope for the French people, Jeanne d'Arc takes to the battlefield and inspires courage to her followers. Starting as a humble villager, Jeanne begins the Journey of a Hero in the Dark Age. Experience forges Jeanne into a powerful leader, gaining access to trusted companions and powerful abilities which shape the empire she was destined to create.

At the confluence of continents, where trade and science meet, and where empires clash, the Ayyubids use their pursuit of knowledge to gain the upper hand. The House of Wisdom is the focal point of this combination of urbanization and science, unlocking powerful Golden Age bonuses and advancing through the ages. The Ayyubids focus on versatile units to gain a tactical advantage on the battlefield.

• Camel units reduce damage from nearby enemy horse cavalry by -20%.
• Mills constructed near Berry Bushes create Orchards, increasing Food capacity by +100.
• Gather from Berry Bushes +30% faster but cannot gather from Boar.
• Berry carry capacity increased +3.
• Land Traders are -33% cheaper.
• Docks are -50% cheaper.
• Advance to the next Age without the need for Villagers by upgrading wings on the House of Wisdom.
• Enter a Golden Age by constructing buildings in the House of Wisdom's influence, increasing resource gather rate, research speed, and production speed.

Advance the ages by upgrading up to 4 Wings on the House of Wisdom.\r\n\r\nGain access to a variety of bonuses for Economy, Technology and Military to reshape the civilization to the needs of the battle.
Infantry can construct Siege Engines on the field without the need for Siege Engineering technology.\r\n\r\nAccess to two unique siege weapons and one unique siege upgrade.

Manjaniq: Unique Mangonel which can use an Incendiary mode to deal damage in a greatly increased area.

Tower of the Sultan: Behemoth siege weapon crewed by archers and equipped with a battering ram for devastating damage against buildings.

Founded to protect the meek and weary, the Knights Templar establish Commanderies throughout Europe to call on their allies. Fielding a spectacular array of heavy troops, the Templars lead the charge into battle with discipline and heart. Fortresses dot the carefully cultivated landscape, providing key defensive positions and safe haven to the pilgrims they protect.

The House of Lancaster emerges as a powerful branch of English royalty. Manors are the backbone of the economy by providing sustainable economic practices. Lancaster military might is of technological advantage and superior tactics on the battlefield. Dismantle enemy formations with potent shock troops then pick them apart with superior ranged infantry.
- Increases civilian unit health by +%1%%% and move speed by +%3%%%. Increases healing from all sources by +%2%%%.

• Age Up at the Town Center by establishing a Commanderie.
• Commanderies permanently unlock units and bonuses.
• Pilgrims generate gold by traveling to Sacred Sites.
• Build Fortresses in the Feudal Age.
• Fortresses increase the range of nearby units and health of nearby Stone Walls.
• Fortresses act as Landmarks, but can be fully destroyed.
• Lumberjacks deposit wood automatically and gather faster with each Age.
• Siege Workshops and Siege Engines cost less wood.
• Trebuchets fire an additional projectile.

Templar Brother: Knight replacement. More expensive than a regular Knight, but has additional health and damage.

Feudal Age

Knight Hospitaller Commanderie - Hospitaller Knight: Heavy Melee Infantry that can heal nearby friendly units.

Kingdom of France Commanderie - Chevalier Confrere: Heavy Cavalry that is cheaper than a regular Knight. Gains additional health for each nearby Heavy Cavalry unit.

Principality of Antioch Commanderie - Serjeant: Heavy Infantry with high melee armor. Throws axes which deal melee damage from range.

Castle Age

Angevin Empire Commanderie - Heavy Spearman: Armored Spearman that is stronger around Fortresses.

Republic of Genoa Commanderie - Genoese Crossbowman: Expensive Crossbowman that deals additional damage to melee infantry.

Kingdom of Castile Commanderie - Genitour: Mounted Javelineer that excels against ranged units.

Imperial Age

Kingdom of Poland Commanderie - Szlachta Cavalry: Elite Cavalry with exceptional ranged armor. Slows the attacks of enemies it damages and deals additional damage to light infantry.

Teutonic Order Commanderie - Teutonic Knight: Absurdly Powerful Heavy Infantry that gains power for each enemy felled in combat. Reduces the armor of nearby enemies.

Republic of Venice Commanderie - Condottiero: Fast infantry that costs only gold. Strong against Gunpowder units.

Venetian Galley: Large oared vessel with a long range forward mounted Trebuchet.

Venetian Trader: Expensive but fast moving trader. Max Build limit of %1%.
Age Up at the Town Center by establishing one of three unique Commanderies. Each Commanderie permanently unlocks a unique unit and bonus.

The Knights Templar cannot train Traders, instead they generate gold from successful pilgrimages. Upon reaching the Feudal Age, the Knights Templar may offer Safe Passage for Pilgrims to travel to a Sacred Site.

Every %1% seconds, a group of Pilgrims will travel from the Templar Headquarters to the selected Sacred Site, generating gold upon arrival based on the distance traveled. The size of the group is increased by %2% for each active Fortress up to a maximum of %3%.

Sacred Sites and Relics are revealed at the start of the game.

My brother the king stands alone in Jerusalem.
While our erstwhile allies march to war in Syria, only a few loyal Knights Templar remain.
The sultan Saladin has brought a vast army to take advantage of our isolation and seize our lands.
He believes our king frail, and our armies meagre.
But this king does not cower behind city walls.
It is time to reclaim the outlying fortifications from Saladin’s men, and banish them from our lands.
Today my brother will force Saladin, and the world, to remember the name of King Baldwin.
Saladin’s army has captured four key locations outside of Jerusalem.
If he continues to control the countryside, he will ruin the city’s farmland and supply chain.
We must take back all four of these key points, before it is too late for our city.
King Baldwin’s bold offensive has shocked Saladin into action.
The sultan is rallying more troops for a counterattack!
Gains +%1% Max Health for each nearby Heavy Cavalry.
Soldiers serving the sultan Baibars have ravaged the countryside for weeks.
Though we have tried to stop him, we’ve learned today that it was all a ruse.
With our forces hunting him elsewhere, Baibars is marching directly on our citadel at Safed.
We’ve held strong against their numbers so far, but Baibars now has siege weapons firing upon our defenses.
If we are to survive, we must sally forth and find supplies to repair our citadel.
And God willing, we should seize any chance we have to destroy those siege weapons.
If we prevail, we may survive long enough for reinforcements to save us.
Baibars has his siege engines firing on our fortress.
We must secure a supply of stone to perform repairs.
If the citadel survives long enough, we may be able to reach the enemy camp and stop the bombardment.
Our workers have protected the fortress from the worst of the assault.
But the enemy has noticed their efforts, and are redoubling their attacks!
Though he descends from Isabella of France, Henry V’s claim to the French throne has been rejected by their court, who refuse to acknowledge inheritance through a woman.
Our king has landed in Normandy with the strength of arms to back his claim.
But after a summer of warfare, our army must retire to a friendly port to rest and resupply.
The enemy commander, Charles d’Albret, has his men harassing us every step of the way.
But the French refuse to engage us directly; even they respect our longbows.
I will assign you a battalion of these peerless archers, and you will see why.
If we can goad the French into attacking us, the strength of our king’s claim will fall upon them with swift and deadly force.
Charles d’Albret is massing his troops in three divisions, and has sent raiders to harass us.
We must set up camp and arm enough soldiers to strike back before they whittle us down.
If we take out the French divisions one by one, we may stand a chance at beating them.
Our boldness has injured the enemy’s pride!
They have redoubled their efforts to defeat us. We must fight on!
The Battle of Towton was a bloody day for our House of Lancaster.
We can hardly count the losses among our allies, our soldiers, and our family.
But we are not yet defeated, for the King and Queen have escaped to the north.
Queen Margaret now sails to recruit aid from Europe, while we try to stem the Yorkist tide at home.
In order to survive, we must rely on the goodwill of the common folk.
The Yorkists attack our people in their very homes.
If we can protect them, I truly believe they will protect us in turn.
While we pray Queen Margaret will rally support to our cause, we must also stand strong, and defend what is still ours.
The Yorkists are attacking towns where we still have support.
If they can, they may even attempt to destroy our stronghold.
We must protect these towns, and our castle, for as long as possible.
We’ve held out longer than anyone expected.
The Yorkists are fed up, and one of their barons is preparing a massive strike against us.

Everyone wants a piece of the Byzantine Empire. Among the crashing waves, peal of the gulls, and many treasures of the Mediterranean coast lie an equal number of dangers; Pirates, cheirosiphons, and the crushing rush of the cataphracts.

"Born in 1161, King Baldwin IV of Jerusalem first developed symptoms of leprosy as a child. The royal physicians suspected his condition, but it was not confirmed until he had already succeeded his father to the throne. The disease would go on to severely disfigure his face and hands, and it ultimately led to his early death at only 24 years old.

Documented evidence of leprosy in humans dates back at least four millennia, and its victims have long faced unique and tremendous social stigma - despite the fact that leprosy has a low transmission rate, even to those directly exposed to the causative bacteria. This stigma was powerful in the 12th century all across the vast spectrum of cultural and religious groups of Eurasia. Cities in many cultures created designated areas where victims of the disease would live in segregation, often referred to as “leper colonies.”

Of course, social class is a powerful factor in the impacts of disease. For King Baldwin, the diagnosis decisively ended his marriage prospects, and caused the court of Jerusalem to pin its hopes of succession on his sister Sibylla; but he continued to live among his family, and rule his country. For commoners in many medieval cultures, leprosy could not only force its victim into living in total segregation, but could damage the marriage and business prospects of their entire extended family.

In the face of this stigma, Baldwin did not hide himself or his illness. He kept his face and hands visible to those around him, even though this caused some subjects to feel uncomfortable around him. He was an active ruler, and despite what onlookers and enemies might have assumed, for a period of his life he was an able horseback rider, and participated in warfare alongside his generals."

"During the Crusades, the various Crusader states often recruited local soldiers. This practice was begun by the Byzantine Empire, who referred to them as “toerkopoeloi“, meaning “sons of Turks.” Among the Knights Templar and Hospitaller, leaders of these local auxilliary units received the title of Turcopolier.

These men served in all roles in the army, but were especially prominent as light cavalry and mounted archers, complementing the strength of the heavily armoured Crusader knights. In some battles they numbered in the thousands, supplementing the Crusader armies with manpower that simply could not be filled entirely with Frankish soldiers.

The turcopoles had complex and varied relationships to religion, national allegiance, and local culture. Some were part of Christian ethnicities or minorities living in the region; others were part of peasant levies; still others were converts. As such, their loyalties and relations to Crusader and Islamic military actions were equally complex and varied.

Sometimes, turcopoles collaborated with Islamic forces to undermine their Crusader masters; this occurred at Safed, where the Syrian Christian castellan of the fortress negotiated with Baibars for safe passage for the Syrians in the garrison, in exchange for tricking the Knights Templar into exiting the fortress. In other cases, loyal turcopoles were considered apostates by Islamic forces, and were executed en masse; this likely happened after the Battle of Hattin, and was later a common Mamluk policy."

"While the crushing defeat of the French at Agincourt is often attributed to the power of the English longbow and the highly unfavourable environmental conditions faced by the French, another technological factor often goes unsung: the humble palings erected by English troops.

Palings were essentially long wooden stakes hammered into the ground and sharpened to a fine point, aimed to face expected oncoming cavalry. Historians still debate exactly what arrangement the stakes were placed in, but they certainly created a formidable barrier to horses.

In many cases, palings forced the cavalry riders to either veer off, or to dismount and attempt to clear the palings by hand. In the worst of cases cavalry would attempt to charge through, and horses and riders would be severely injured or killed by impacts with the palings.

These were a surprise to the French at Agincourt, and a new innovation for the English. It is thought to have been inspired by Ottoman tactics used at the Battle of Nicopolis in 1396, an event fresh in the minds of military thinkers in Europe in the early 15th century.

After the tremendous victory at Agincourt, the English began to regularly use palings to protect their archers against cavalry charges, and continued to do so well into the 16th century."

"Initially referred to as The Civil Wars, the conflict between the Houses of Lancaster and York was later termed the War of the Roses by 19th century Victorian writers. This name refers to the heraldic badges of the two houses, both of which were distinct roses.

The White Rose of York is thought to have been chosen because of the religious connotations of the colour white, which at the time was closely linked to the Virgin Mary, and symbolized such traits as purity, joy, and glory. The real white rose of York, Rosa × alba, is a hybrid of unknown origins that has been grown in Europe for many centuries.

The Red Rose of Lancaster, meanwhile, is thought to represent a specific cultivar of Rosa gallica, the Gallic rose, which is possibly the oldest cultivated species of rose in the world. The House of Lancaster only truly began to use the Red Rose as a wartime symbol near the very end of the conflict, inspired to present a counterpart to the rose of York.

Eventually the Lancastrian Henry Tudor married Elizabeth of York, ending the war. He chose to combine the two heraldic roses into a new Tudor Rose, which features a small white rose inset in a larger red rose. This new rose became the heraldic symbol of the House of Tudor, and Henry VII became its first king."

Earning a better score will require bold action. Focus on producing combat units to counter the enemy's forces and meet them on the battlefield to seize victory more quickly. The French commanders wield powerful support abilities, but each division can be overcome with the right tactics.

Set up defenses at each town when the Yorkists begin their assault, and rely on your allies when necessary to bolster your forces. By exploring the map, you can find hidden resources and reinforcements that will aid in your defense. Spend your resources producing units, and advance to later Ages to keep up with increasing enemy attacks.

Make sure to create more military production buildings to replenish your army. The Headquarters tent will start producing units if you capture the nearby Sacred Site; you can use this to create a defense garrison to protect the area around the site. Reinforcing enemy positions you capture will make sure the Sacred Sites are well protected against Ayyubids attacks. The Point of Interests are partially revealed on your minimap, completing them can give you more resources to overcome the enemy forces.

Time is against you as you flee Safed with your villagers and military to gather stone for fortress repairs. A villager repair crew (team yellow) will stay behind to repair the fortress once you collect stone. The path to each stone mine is dangerous, and you will face Mamluk forces along the way. Add your villagers and military to their own Control Groups for easier selection and commands. When you encounter an attack, use the soldiers best suited to deal with the enemy force. Keep your villagers protected, as the more that survive and mine stone, the more stone is acquired. While mining stone, watch all sides for incoming threats and be ready to defend your villagers. When gifted supplies to train new units, diversify your unit composition as evenly as possible.
The Ayyubids will periodically test your defenses by launching attacks on your positions.
The Ayyubid forces around Sacred Sites will get stronger with time.

• Gold deposited by Villagers generates bonus Stone and vice versa.
• Extra melee damage upgrade accessible in the Dark Age (I).
• Ikko-ikki are combat monks that can be recruited from Buddhist Monasteries that heal while attacking.
• Keeps come equipped with Rocket Emplacements and increased health in the Imperial Age (IV).
• Fishing Boats are cheaper to produce.
• The location of all Deer and Boar are revealed on the map.

Daimyo Estates are unique buildings that can be dedicated to one of three clans. Hojo clan which improves melee infantry, Oda clan which improves ranged infantry, or Takeda clan which improves cavalry. The Daimyo Estate also has an influence that increases production speed of military buildings.

• Yatai: Worker unit that will passively collect food from nearby Food sources without killing or consuming them.

• Yari Cavalry: Fast cavalry effective at raiding, flanking, and countering ranged units. Bonus damage against cavalry.

• Daimyo: High health and damage melee cavalry that provides an aura that increases attack speed of nearby military units. Effective against melee units. Limited to one per Daimyo Estate built.

• Yumi Ashigaru: Archer with improved move speed for reduced cost and health.

• Ikko-ikki Monk: Warrior Monk with strong combat capabilities that heals nearby units when attacking. Does not have a targeted heal ability.

• Kanabo Samurai: Tough infantry with good damage. Deals bonus damage based on the max health of the enemy.

• Early Ozutsu: Heavy ranged gunpowder infantry that has a huge handheld cannon with high siege and splash damage.

The Matsuri is a unique early Market that can produce Yatai. The Yatai is a unique worker unit that will slowly generate food from Food sources within %1% tiles without killing or consuming them. If the Yatai collects enough Food it will send a Food Trader back to the Matsuri which improves an aura that increases the gather rate of nearby villagers.

High health and damage melee cavalry that provides an aura that increases attack speed of nearby military units. Effective against melee units. Limited to one per Daimyo Estate built.

• Start with maximum population limit.
• Units are always recruited in batches.
• Stockyards are a unique building that can be worked by 4 villagers and is a food drop-off. 
• Ovoos can be built on multiple Stone deposits increasing Stone income.
• Unique technologies costing only Stone.  
• Stables available in the Dark Age (I).
• Age up by building and upgrading the Golden Tent.
• Fortified Outposts provide influence or aura bonuses based on the Edict set in the Golden Tent.
• Silk Road: Traders generate +10% additional Food, Wood and Gold with 5/10/15 active Traders.
• Transport ships have +50% health and +15% move speed.
• Scouts can deploy the Scouting Falcon ability to keep watch over the surrounding area.

The Golden Tent is the only landmark for the Golden Horde, which can be upgraded to advance to the next age and increase the health of the building. An edict can be chosen on the Golden Tent, which changes the influence or aura bonus provided by Fortified Outposts.\r\n\r\nThe Batu Khan and Torguud can be recruited from the Golden Tent.
Periodically spawns raiders. Destroy to prevent raids from this location.
Units are produced in batches of two. Technologies can be researched that improve batch production.\r\n
Construct the Stockyard instead of Farms or Pastures for Food — Stockyards have Sheep carcasses which can be gathered and dropped off at the Stockyard directly.
Fortified Outposts have more health and provide influence or aura bonuses depending on the Edict chosen on the Golden Tent.
Construct Ovoo over Stone outcroppings to harvest Stone without the need for Villagers. The maximum number of Ovoos that can be built is increased +%1% per age up.

Golden Horde Khan: Heavy Cavalry that scales in strength on age up. Can fire an arrow that increases the damage taken by enemies in an area. The Khan will also provide an increasing attack damage aura the longer the Khan is in combat.

Torguud: Heavy Cavalry which can protect the Khan, redirecting any damage taken by the Khan to the Torguud.

Kharash: Weak Light Melee Infantry units that provide armor to nearby allies.

Kipchak: Horse archer that can fire arrows which cause the target hit to bleed.

Traction Trebuchet: Faster move speed, setup time and attack speed.

Keshik: Heavy Cavalry available in Feudal Age, regenerates Health after every attack.

Welcome to The Crucible! A challenge of endurance, strategy, and luck. 


Hold out against endless waves in dynamic missions that change every time you play.


All roads lead to your Wonder, and the enemy is coming…

Your Wonder is the key to victory. It must be protected at all costs. 

Earn medals by surviving as long as possible. 

Enemy attacks will escalate as the mission progresses.

Boons are powerful upgrades and abilities that you can select during a mission. 

You will periodically be offered a selection to choose from. 

Build a unique set of Boons to crush your enemies.

Every mission has two optional objectives that are chosen randomly. 

Objectives are revealed at the start of the mission. 

Complete them to earn powerful rewards.
Points of interest are smaller optional challenges that are hidden around the map. \r\n\r\nFind them and defeat their guardians to claim their rewards.

Use Perk Points to unlock permanent benefits between missions. 

Earn Perk Points by playing missions. The longer you survive, the more you earn. 

Take on greater challenges as your power grows.

Atgeirmaðr: Spearman replacement. Light melee infantry with a passive ability that increases their attack speed when Varangian units are nearby.

Emperor Basil II employed Varangians to defend against revolts fomented by Byzantine nobles. Arriving on ships from the north, these foreign-born warriors were not swayed by Byzantine politics and culture, creating an elite shock division protecting emperors for centuries to come. The Macedonian Dynasty mints Silver as a unique resource to spend at Varangian Arsenals, sharpening their blades and honing their skills while they protect the Emperor.
Villagers gather Berry Bushes 30% faster but cannot gather from Boar.\r\nVillagers carry +3 Food from Berry Bushes.

Take the load off your Villagers back with Worker Elephants. These elephants act as a universal drop-off and can move around freely, saving time and resources for Villagers to focus on working.\r\n\r\nWorker Elephants, Town Centers, and Docks provide bonus resources when Villagers and Fishing Ships drop off.

Strike fear into your enemies with the Tughlaqabad Fort. Available from the Feudal Age (II), this defensive building can be upgraded to increase its damage output, health, and unlocks your choice in a Governor.

Choose between one of any six Governors at the Tughlaqabad Fort to help lead the Tughlaq Dynasty to new heights. Governor strength increases with each Tughlaqabad Fort improvement upgrade. Governors provide economy, tempo, military, research, and religious bonuses. \r\n\r\nOne Governor may be active per fort.

Raiding Elephant: Fast elephant effective at raiding, flanking, and countering ranged units. Can trample to bypass any unit to deal damage in an area.

Healer Elephant: High health and armored melee elephant mounted with two Imams that can heal units individually. Deals more damage for each nearby melee infantry.

War Elephant: Heavy melee cavalry mounted with a Spearman that has high health and damage with a strong siege attack.

Ballista Elephant: Ranged elephant mounted with a ballista thats effective against melee units and can pierce multiple targets.

• Town Centers, Worker Elephants, and Docks provide bonus resources when Villagers and Fishing Ships drop off.
• Worker Elephants move freely and act as universal resource drop-offs. 
• All technology is more expensive but research near instantly.
• Tughlaqabad Forts act as Keeps and are available from the Feudal Age (II). 
• Tughlaqabad Forts can be upgraded to increase defenses and unlocks Governors.
• Select a Governor at any fort to take the dynasty to new heights.
• Governors provide economy, tempo, military, research, and religious bonuses.
• Infantry units are able to construct Palisade Walls.
• Gather from Berry Bushes +30% faster but cannot gather from Boar.
• Berry carry capacity increased +3.
• Fishing Ships can defend themselves with a ranged attack.

The Sengoku Jidai was an era of shifting alliances and ceaseless warfare. Three rival clans can be supported to gain powerful advantages to your ranged infantry, melee infantry or cavalry. Even in the midst of war there was time for celebration; the Matsuri festival is the heart of commerce and trade providing powerful bonuses to their economy.

Leading the Golden Horde westwards, Batu Khan expanded the Mongolian Empire right into the heart of Eastern Europe. The Khan’s great Golden Tent was the center of network of control, with outposts that allowed them to maintain a vast territory. While still powerful raiders, the Golden Horde sacrificed the mobility of their camps to field larger forces needed to control their vassal states.
